title: は — The Topic Particle
content: は marks the topic of a sentence — what the sentence is about. It is pronounced 'wa' in this grammatical role, not 'ha'. Japanese conversation frequently omits the subject when it is understood from context, so は carries the weight of establishing what is being discussed. It is not the same as が, which marks the grammatical subject and emphasises it.
example_one: 私は学生です。(わたしはがくせいです。) — I am a student.
example_two: 猫は動物です。(ねこはどうぶつです。) — A cat is an animal.
supporting_content: は can also signal contrast between two things: 魚は食べます。(さかなはたべます。) — As for fish, I will eat it (implying I will not eat something else).

title: の — Connection of Nouns
content: の connects nouns to show possession or specificity. It works like the English 's or the word 'of', but in Japanese it always flows from the possessor to the thing possessed. You can chain multiple nouns with の for greater specificity. Japanese has no direct equivalent of 'of' as a standalone word — the relationship is built into の itself.
example_one: たけしさんの車 (たけしさんのくるま) — Takeshi's car
example_two: 日本語の本 (にほんごのほん) — A Japanese-language book
supporting_content: Reversing の changes the meaning entirely. 車のたけし (くるまのたけし) would produce a nonsensical phrase — something like 'Takeshi made of car'.

title: も — The Particle for Similar Qualities
content: も replaces は or が after a noun to express 'also' or 'too'. It indicates that the new subject shares the same quality or situation as something already mentioned in the conversation.
example_one: 田中さんは学生です。山田さんも学生です。(たなかさんはがくせいです。やまださんもがくせいです。) — Mr. Tanaka is a student. Ms. Yamada is also a student.
example_two: これも高いです。(これもたかいです。) — This is expensive too.
supporting_content: も replaces は or が entirely. Never use はも or がも together. The particle being replaced determines the position of も in the sentence.

title: か — Question Sentences with the Particle か
content: か added to the end of any statement turns it into a yes-or-no question — no word order change is needed. か can also combine with the question word なん to form open questions. なん typically appears before ですか or before a counter word such as じ or さい. The alternate form なに appears before a particle instead of a counter.
example_one: これは本ですか。(これはほんですか。) — Is this a book?
example_two: なんじですか。(なんじですか。) — What time is it?
supporting_content: In casual speech, か is often replaced with rising intonation. In polite registers, always keep か at the end of the sentence.

title: が — The Subject Marker Particle
content: が marks the grammatical subject of a sentence. While は marks the topic, が emphasises the subject itself — especially for new information, answers to who or what questions, and in specific grammar patterns such as existence and desire. The key distinction is: は sets a topic the speaker wants to discuss; が pinpoints exactly who or what performs or receives an action.
example_one: 誰が来ましたか。田中さんが来ました。(だれがきましたか。たなかさんがきました。) — Who came? Tanaka came.
example_two: 猫がいます。(ねこがいます。) — There is a cat.
supporting_content: が is required with certain adjectives expressing desire, ability, and preferences: 水が飲みたい (みずがのみたい) — I want to drink water. Learning when が replaces は will deepen naturally with exposure.

title: です — Ending Sentences with Desu
content: です ends a sentence in the polite present tense to assert a state of being — equivalent to 'is', 'am', or 'are' in English. It follows nouns and な-adjectives. です itself carries no literal content; it is a copula that makes the sentence polite and complete.
example_one: 私は先生です。(わたしはせんせいです。) — I am a teacher.
example_two: 今日は月曜日です。(きょうはげつようびです。) — Today is Monday.
supporting_content: です does not conjugate verbs — it only follows nouns and な-adjectives. For verbs, use the verb's own polite ending ます.

title: Negative Forms of です
content: There are multiple negative forms of です depending on the register you need. じゃないです is the casual spoken form. じゃありません is the conservative spoken form. ではありません is the formal written form. All three mean 'is not'.
example_one: これは本じゃないです。(これはほんじゃないです。) — This is not a book.
example_two: 田中さんは学生ではありません。(たなかさんはがくせいではありません。) — Mr. Tanaka is not a student.
supporting_content: じゃ is a spoken contraction of では (dewa). In very formal writing or official documents, always use ではありません.

title: Seeking Confirmation with ~ね
content: ね is a sentence-ending particle that seeks agreement or confirmation from the listener — similar to 'right?', 'isn't it?', or 'don't you think?' in English. It softens a statement and invites the listener to share the observation. It can also express gentle shared emotion.
example_one: 今日は暑いですね。(きょうはあついですね。) — It's hot today, isn't it?
example_two: このケーキ、おいしいですね。(このけーき、おいしいですね。) — This cake is delicious, isn't it?
supporting_content: ね is very common in everyday conversation. Overusing it can make you sound uncertain, so reserve it for when you genuinely want to check understanding or share a feeling.

title: Expressing Confidence with ~よ
content: よ is a sentence-ending particle that asserts information the speaker believes the listener does not yet know. It conveys confidence and emphasis — similar to 'I'm telling you' or a firm exclamation point. Unlike ね, which seeks agreement, よ pushes new information toward the listener.
example_one: このお店はおいしいですよ。(このおみせはおいしいですよ。) — This restaurant is good, I'm telling you.
example_two: もう九時ですよ。(もうくじですよ。) — It's already 9 o'clock, you know.
supporting_content: よ and ね can be combined as よね, which asserts while still seeking mild confirmation: おいしいですよね。— It's delicious, right? (I think so and expect you'll agree.)

title: これ・それ・あれ・どれ — This / That / That Over There / Which
content: These are standalone pointer words used with は to reference an object without naming it. これ refers to an object close to the speaker. それ refers to an object close to the listener. あれ refers to an object far from both. どれ asks 'which one?' and is always used in questions. Do not use these to refer to people nearby — it is considered very impolite.
example_one: これは何ですか。(これはなんですか。) — What is this?
example_two: あれは田中さんの車です。(あれはたなかさんのくるまです。) — That over there is Tanaka's car.
supporting_content: どれ always appears in a question sentence: どれがあなたの傘ですか。(どれがあなたのかさですか。) — Which one is your umbrella?

title: この・その・あの・どの — Pointer Words with a Noun
content: Unlike these/that/those standalone forms, この/その/あの/どの must be followed directly by a noun to specify which one. この refers to a noun near the speaker. その refers to a noun near the listener. あの refers to a noun far from both. どの asks 'which ~?' and is used in questions.
example_one: この本は面白いです。(このほんはおもしろいです。) — This book is interesting.
example_two: どの電車に乗りますか。(どのでんしゃにのりますか。) — Which train do you take?
supporting_content: この/その/あの/どの cannot stand alone — they always need a noun after them. Use これ/それ/あれ/どれ when you are not following with a noun.

title: ここ・そこ・あそこ・どこ — Here / There / Over There / Where
content: These pointer words describe location rather than objects. ここ means 'here', near the speaker. そこ means 'there', near the listener. あそこ means 'over there', far from both. どこ means 'where?' and is used in questions. Unlike object pointer words, these typically appear with です or ですか at the end of a sentence.
example_one: トイレはどこですか。(トイレはどこですか。) — Where is the restroom?
example_two: 駅はあそこです。(えきはあそこです。) — The station is over there.
supporting_content: ここ can also indicate your current place of residence or work in context: ここに五年住んでいます。— I have lived here for five years.

title: だれ・だれの — Who and Whose
content: だれ means 'who' and is used without a following noun in question sentences. だれの means 'whose' and must be followed by a noun to ask about possession.
example_one: あの人はだれですか。(あのひとはだれですか。) — Who is that person?
example_two: これはだれの本ですか。(これはだれのほんですか。) — Whose book is this?
supporting_content: In formal or written Japanese, どなた is a more polite equivalent of だれ: どなたですか。— Who are you? (politely). Use どなた with customers, superiors, or strangers.

title: なに・なん — What (Question Word)
content: なに and なん both mean 'what', but the form used depends on what follows. Use なん before です, a counter word, or の. Use なに before a particle such as を, が, or か, or at the end of a sentence.
example_one: これは何ですか。(これはなんですか。) — What is this?
example_two: 何を食べますか。(なにをたべますか。) — What will you eat?
supporting_content: なんさいですか uses なん before the counter さい (How old are you?). なにがすきですか uses なに before the particle が (What do you like?). The rule is simple: counter or です → なん; particle → なに.

title: います・あります — Stating Existence for Animate and Inanimate Objects
content: To state that something exists, use が (not は) for the subject and に (not で) for the location. Place the location first, as is common practice. あります is for non-living things. います is for living things such as people and animals. These verbs also express ownership or that an event will take place.
example_one: 机の上に本があります。(つくえのうえにほんがあります。) — There is a book on top of the desk.
example_two: 公園に子供がいます。(こうえんにこどもがいます。) — There are children in the park.
supporting_content: To express ownership: 車があります。(くるまがあります。) — I have a car. To express an upcoming event: パーティーがあります。— There will be a party.

title: Describing Where Things Are
content: Japanese has a set of location words used with の and a reference noun to describe where something is. These words follow the reference noun and precede に or で. The main location words are: みぎ (right), ひだり (left), まえ (in front), うしろ (behind), なか (inside), うえ (above/on top), した (below/under), ちかく or そば (near/beside), となり (next to, adjacent).
example_one: 本は机の上にあります。(ほんはつくえのうえにあります。) — The book is on top of the desk.
example_two: 猫はソファーの下にいます。(ねこはそふぁーのしたにいます。) — The cat is under the sofa.
supporting_content: Combine these with います/あります from Lesson 15: [object]は [reference noun]の[location]に[います/あります]。The location word always follows の and the reference noun.

title: Pronunciation — Pitch Accent
content: Japanese uses pitch accent rather than stress accent. Each mora (unit of sound) is either high or low in pitch. There are four patterns: Flat (Heiban) — starts low, rises after the first mora, and stays high; Head-high (Atamadaka) — starts high and drops after the first mora; Middle-high (Nakadaka) — rises and then drops somewhere in the middle; Tail-high (Odaka) — rises across the word and drops on the following particle.
example_one: 橋 (はし) head-high = chopsticks; 橋 (はし) flat = bridge. The spelling is identical but pitch changes the meaning.
example_two: 雨 (あめ) head-high = rain; 飴 (あめ) flat = candy. Pitch accent distinguishes these homophones.
supporting_content: Pitch accent varies by dialect. Tokyo (standard) pitch is what is typically taught. Learners may not be penalised for wrong pitch, but training your ear early helps with listening comprehension and natural speech.

title: Requesting Things from People
content: To politely ask for a physical item, follow the object with をください after the object being requested. An alternative is をおねがいします, also placed after the object, which carries a slightly more pleading tone. をおねがいします can also be used to request abstract things such as services, explanations, or understanding.
example_one: 水をください。(みずをください。) — Please give me water.
example_two: 説明をおねがいします。(せつめいをおねがいします。) — Please give me an explanation.
supporting_content: おねがいします can stand alone as a general 'please' when making a request without specifying the object. It is one of the most universally useful polite expressions in Japanese.

title: To Offer an Object to Another Person
content: To politely offer an object to someone, follow the object with をどうぞ. どうぞ alone also works as a welcoming gesture meaning 'please, go ahead' or 'here you are'. It requires no verb and is used in a wide range of offering and welcoming situations.
example_one: コーヒーをどうぞ。(こーひーをどうぞ。) — Please have some coffee.
example_two: どうぞ、お座りください。(どうぞ、おすわりください。) — Please, go ahead and sit down.
supporting_content: どうぞ by itself is used when handing something over, holding a door, or ushering someone into a room. It is one of the most natural single-word expressions in everyday Japanese.

title: Verb Conjugation — Ru-Verbs
content: Ru-verbs (also called ichidan or Group 2 verbs) always end in る in dictionary form and their stem never changes. To conjugate politely, drop る and add the appropriate ending. Present affirmative polite: stem + ます. Present negative polite: stem + ません.
example_one: 食べる → 食べます (たべます) — eat (polite affirmative) / 食べません (たべません) — do not eat.
example_two: 見る → 見ます (みます) — see (polite affirmative) / 見ません (みません) — do not see.
supporting_content: Most ru-verbs end in える or いる sounds. However some u-verbs also end in る such as 帰る (かえる) and 走る (はしる) — these must be memorised as exceptions.

title: Verb Conjugation — U-Verbs
content: U-verbs (godan or Group 1 verbs) end in a variety of う-row sounds: う, く, ぐ, す, つ, ぬ, ぶ, む, or る. To make the polite form, change the final sound to its い-row equivalent and add ます or ません.
example_one: 書く → 書きます (かきます) — write (affirmative) / 書きません (かきません) — do not write.
example_two: 飲む → 飲みます (のみます) — drink (affirmative) / 飲みません (のみません) — do not drink.
supporting_content: Conjugation changes: く→き, ぐ→ぎ, す→し, つ→ち, ぬ→に, ぶ→び, む→み, う→い, る(u-verb)→り. The key skill is identifying whether a る-ending verb is a ru-verb or u-verb.

title: Verb Conjugation — Irregular Verbs
content: Japanese has only two truly irregular verbs: する (to do) and くる (to come). These do not follow ru-verb or u-verb patterns and must be memorised. Present affirmative polite: します, きます. Present negative polite: しません, きません.
example_one: 毎日勉強します。(まいにちべんきょうします。) — I study every day.
example_two: 友達が来ます。(ともだちがきます。) — My friend is coming.
supporting_content: Many compound verbs are formed with する attached to a noun: 運動する (to exercise), 料理する (to cook), 説明する (to explain). All conjugate exactly like する.

title: Action Verbs Explained
content: Japanese verbs express both habitual actions (things you regularly do) and future actions (things you will do) using the same present-tense polite form ます. Context and time words distinguish the two meanings. Japanese has no separate future tense — the same form covers present habits and future plans.
example_one: 毎朝コーヒーを飲みます。(まいあさこーひーをのみます。) — I drink coffee every morning. (habitual)
example_two: 明日友達に会います。(あしたともだちにあいます。) — I will meet my friend tomorrow. (future)
supporting_content: Time words like 毎日 (every day), 明日 (tomorrow), and 来週 (next week) signal whether an action is habitual or future. Without time words, context from the conversation clarifies the meaning.

title: を — The Object Particle
content: を marks the direct object of an action verb — the thing that receives the action. It always follows a noun and precedes the verb. を is pronounced 'o' in modern Japanese.
example_one: 本を読みます。(ほんをよみます。) — I read a book.
example_two: 音楽を聞きます。(おんがくをききます。) — I listen to music.
supporting_content: を also marks a space that movement passes through with verbs of motion: 公園を歩きます (こうえんをあるきます) — I walk through the park. The core meaning is always the thing affected by or traversed by the action.

title: で — The Particle for Place of Action
content: で indicates the location where an action takes place — it answers 'where does this happen?' Note the key distinction: で marks where an action is performed, while に marks where something exists or the destination of movement.
example_one: 図書館で勉強します。(としょかんでべんきょうします。) — I study at the library.
example_two: レストランでご飯を食べます。(れすとらんでごはんをたべます。) — I eat a meal at a restaurant.
supporting_content: Quick rule: で = action location (doing something at a place). に = existence location (something is there) or destination (going to a place). Both are extremely common so learning them as a pair is helpful.

title: に — The Particle for Goal of Movement
content: に indicates the destination or goal of movement — where you are going to or arriving at. It follows the destination noun and precedes a movement verb such as いく (go), くる (come), or かえる (return).
example_one: 学校に行きます。(がっこうにいきます。) — I go to school.
example_two: 家に帰ります。(うちにかえります。) — I return home.
supporting_content: Compare に and で: 学校に行きます (going to school) vs 学校で勉強します (studying at school). に marks the endpoint; で marks where you perform the action once you arrive.

title: に — The Particle for Time
content: に marks a specific point in time at which an action occurs. It is used with clock times, days of the week, dates, months, and years. Critically, it is not used with relative time expressions like 今日 (today), 明日 (tomorrow), 毎日 (every day), or 今 (now).
example_one: 七時に起きます。(しちじにおきます。) — I wake up at 7 o'clock.
example_two: 月曜日に会議があります。(げつようびにかいぎがあります。) — There is a meeting on Monday.
supporting_content: No に with: 今日、明日、毎朝、今. Use に with: specific clock times (三時に), days of the week (火曜日に), months (五月に), and years (二〇二四年に).

title: へ — Goal of Movement (More Literary)
content: へ (pronounced 'e') also marks direction or destination of movement, similar to に. However, へ emphasises the direction of travel rather than the specific endpoint, and is more common in formal or written Japanese. In everyday spoken Japanese, に is used more frequently.
example_one: 東京へ行きます。(とうきょうへいきます。) — I go to Tokyo.
example_two: 日本へようこそ。(にほんへようこそ。) — Welcome to Japan.
supporting_content: へ and に are often interchangeable with movement verbs, but only に can mark time or existence location. When in doubt, use に in conversation and reserve へ for formal or written contexts.

title: Extending an Invitation Using ~ませんか
content: ませんか is added to the polite verb stem to extend an invitation or suggestion — 'Would you like to ~?' or 'Won't you ~?'. This is the negative question form, and in Japanese the polite way to invite is phrased negatively, giving the listener an easy way to decline without confrontation.
example_one: 一緒に映画を見ませんか。(いっしょにえいがをみませんか。) — Would you like to watch a movie together?
example_two: 昼ご飯を食べませんか。(ひるごはんをたべませんか。) — Would you like to have lunch?
supporting_content: ませんか is directed at the listener as an invitation. ましょうか (Lesson 45) is a softer proposal — 'Shall we ~?' — and can also offer your own help to someone.

title: Describing Frequency Using Frequency Adverbs
content: Japanese uses adverbs placed before the verb to describe how often an action occurs. Key frequency adverbs: いつも (always), よく (often), 時々 (sometimes), あまり (not very often — requires a negative verb), ぜんぜん (not at all — requires a negative verb).
example_one: 私は時々映画を見ます。(わたしはときどきえいがをみます。) — I sometimes watch movies.
example_two: あまりお酒を飲みません。(あまりおさけをのみません。) — I don't drink alcohol very often.
supporting_content: あまり and ぜんぜん must always pair with a negative verb form. Using them with a positive verb is a grammar error: wrong — あまり食べます; correct — あまり食べません.

title: Flexibility of Japanese Sentences and Common Conventions
content: Japanese word order is flexible because particles mark each word's grammatical role. The verb almost always comes at the end, but nouns and their particles can be rearranged for emphasis. Subjects and topics are frequently omitted when clear from context. Time expressions typically appear near the beginning.
example_one: 明日、私は図書館で本を読みます。— Tomorrow I will read a book at the library. (standard order)
example_two: 本を明日図書館で読みます。— The book — I will read it tomorrow at the library. (rearranged for emphasis)
supporting_content: The verb-final rule is strict. You can move almost anything else around within a clause, but the verb or copula always stays last. This is one of the most important structural rules in Japanese.

title: は — Further Explained
content: は has a second important function beyond marking a topic: contrast. When は appears where が or を might be expected, it signals a contrast with something else, either stated or implied. は can single out one element of a sentence to contrast against an unstated alternative.
example_one: 魚は食べます。(さかなはたべます。) — Fish, I will eat. (implying I will not eat something else such as meat)
example_two: 東京には行きます。大阪には行きません。(とうきょうにはいきます。おおさかにはいきません。) — I will go to Tokyo. I will not go to Osaka.
supporting_content: Particles like に, で, and を can combine with は to create a contrasting emphasis: にはいきます vs にはいきません. This contrastive は is one of the subtler features of Japanese and rewards careful attention.

title: Past Tense of です — Affirmative and Negative
content: To express a past state of being, replace です with でした for the affirmative. For the negative past there are several options: じゃなかったです is casual spoken, じゃありませんでした is conservative spoken, and ではありませんでした is formal written.
example_one: 昨日は雨でした。(きのうはあめでした。) — Yesterday it was rain.
example_two: 彼は学生じゃありませんでした。(かれはがくせいじゃありませんでした。) — He was not a student.
supporting_content: Summary: Affirmative past → でした. Casual negative past → じゃなかったです. Conservative negative past → じゃありませんでした. Formal negative past → ではありませんでした.

title: Past Tense of Verbs — Affirmative and Negative
content: To form the polite past tense of any verb, replace ます with ました for the affirmative, or ませんでした for the negative. This applies to ru-verbs, u-verbs, and irregular verbs alike — once they are in polite form, the ending change is the same for all.
example_one: 昨日映画を見ました。(きのうえいがをみました。) — I watched a movie yesterday.
example_two: 先週勉強しませんでした。(せんしゅうべんきょうしませんでした。) — I did not study last week.
supporting_content: Pattern: verb stem + ました (past affirmative) / ませんでした (past negative). Only the ending changes — the verb stem stays the same as in the present form.

title: も — Alternate Uses
content: Beyond expressing similarity, も appears in quantity expressions and negative statements. Combined with a number and a negative verb, も means 'not even one'. Combined with question words and a negative verb, も creates 'nothing', 'nobody', or 'nowhere'.
example_one: お金が一円もありません。(おかねがいちえんもありません。) — I don't have even one yen.
example_two: 誰も来ませんでした。(だれもきませんでした。) — Nobody came.
supporting_content: Full set: 何も (nothing) + negative, 誰も (nobody) + negative, どこも (nowhere) + negative. These are very natural in everyday Japanese conversation.

title: Duration of Activity — じかん
content: To express how long an action continues, state the time duration before the verb without adding に. The duration expression answers 'for how long?' rather than 'at what time?'. Common time duration units: 時間 (hours), 分 (minutes), 週間 (weeks), ヶ月 (months), 年 (years).
example_one: 三時間勉強しました。(さんじかんべんきょうしました。) — I studied for three hours.
example_two: 一時間待ちました。(いちじかんまちました。) — I waited for one hour.
supporting_content: Duration takes no particle, or optionally を: 三時間(を)勉強する. Do not add に (which marks a specific point in time, not a duration).

title: Expressions of Quantity — たくさん
content: たくさん means 'a lot', 'many', or 'much' and can modify a verb directly or precede の plus a noun. Other quantity expressions: 少し (sukoshi — a little), あまり (not much — with negative verb), ぜんぜん (not at all — with negative verb).
example_one: たくさん食べました。(たくさんたべました。) — I ate a lot.
example_two: たくさんの人が来ました。(たくさんのひとがきました。) — Many people came.
supporting_content: たくさん can precede の plus a noun (たくさんの本 — many books) or follow the object particle を (本をたくさん読む — read many books). Both patterns are correct and common.

title: と — The Companion Particle
content: と has two main uses: listing nouns exhaustively ('A and B') and marking a companion for an action ('together with someone'). As a companion particle, と follows a person's name or pronoun and precedes the verb. と lists all named items completely, unlike や which only gives examples.
example_one: パンと卵を買いました。(ぱんとたまごをかいました。) — I bought bread and eggs.
example_two: 友達と映画を見ました。(ともだちとえいがをみました。) — I watched a movie with my friend.
supporting_content: と lists everything exhaustively (A and B, that is all). や (Lesson 102) gives examples from a larger group (A and B, among other things). Use と when the list is complete; use や when it is not.

title: い-Adjectives in the Present Tense
content: い-adjectives end in い in their dictionary form and conjugate by modifying that ending. Polite affirmative: adjective + です (the い stays). Polite negative: drop い and add くないです or くありません. The base い is retained in the affirmative and changed only in the negative.
example_one: この映画は面白いです。(このえいがはおもしろいです。) — This movie is interesting.
example_two: この部屋は広くないです。(このへやはひろくないです。) — This room is not spacious.
supporting_content: Important exception: いい (good) is irregular. Its negative is よくないです. Its polite form is いいです, but all other conjugations use よ as the base: よくない, よかった, よかったら.

title: な-Adjectives in the Present Tense
content: な-adjectives are grammatically similar to nouns. In predicate position (after は), they are followed by です for the affirmative or じゃないです / じゃありません for the negative. When modifying a noun directly, add な between the adjective and the noun.
example_one: 東京は賑やかです。(とうきょうはにぎやかです。) — Tokyo is lively.
example_two: ここは静かじゃないです。(ここはしずかじゃないです。) — This place is not quiet.
supporting_content: Common な-adjectives: きれい (pretty/clean), 便利 (convenient), 有名 (famous), 元気 (energetic/well), 好き (liked), 嫌い (disliked), 大切 (important). The な they add before nouns is what gives this adjective class its name.

title: い-Adjectives in the Past Tense
content: To make an い-adjective past tense, drop the final い and add かったです for the affirmative, or くなかったです / くありませんでした for the negative.
example_one: 昨日は寒かったです。(きのうはさむかったです。) — It was cold yesterday.
example_two: その映画は面白くなかったです。(そのえいがはおもしろくなかったです。) — That movie was not interesting.
supporting_content: Key exception: いい → past affirmative is よかったです (not いかったです). This is the most commonly used irregular い-adjective form. Forgetting it is one of the most common learner mistakes.

title: な-Adjectives in the Past Tense
content: な-adjectives form the past tense in the same way nouns do with です. Past affirmative: adjective + でした. Past negative: adjective + じゃなかったです or じゃありませんでした.
example_one: 子供の頃、東京は静かでした。(こどものころ、とうきょうはしずかでした。) — When I was a child, Tokyo was quiet.
example_two: その仕事は大変じゃなかったです。(そのしごとはたいへんじゃなかったです。) — That job was not difficult.
supporting_content: な-adjective past forms are identical in structure to noun past forms. Both use でした (past affirmative) and じゃなかったです / じゃありませんでした (past negative).

title: Modifying Nouns with Adjectives
content: Adjectives can directly precede a noun to describe it. い-adjectives simply come before the noun with no change. な-adjectives add な between the adjective and the noun.
example_one: 大きい犬 (おおきいいぬ) — a big dog / きれいな花 (きれいなはな) — a beautiful flower
example_two: 新しい車を買いました。(あたらしいくるまをかいました。) — I bought a new car.
supporting_content: Pattern: い-adjective → [adjective]い + noun. な-adjective → [adjective]な + noun. Never add な to an い-adjective or drop い when using it before a noun.

title: Like / Dislike / Neutrality — すき・きらい
content: To express likes and dislikes, use the な-adjective 好き (liked) or 嫌い (disliked) after the thing you like or dislike, which is marked with が. For neutrality, use すきでもきらいでもない. Note that these adjectives use が — not を — for the thing liked or disliked.
example_one: 私は猫が好きです。(わたしはねこがすきです。) — I like cats.
example_two: 辛い食べ物が嫌いです。(からいたべものがきらいです。) — I dislike spicy food.
supporting_content: 大好き (daisuki — really love) and 大嫌い (daikirai — really hate) are more emphatic versions. Use them with care. The structure は〜が好きです/嫌いです is one of the most essential sentence patterns.

title: Suggesting a Plan of Action — ましょう・ましょうか
content: ましょう is added to the polite verb stem to suggest doing something together — 'Let's ~'. ましょうか softens it into a collaborative question — 'Shall we ~?'. Both are warm and inclusive in tone, making them ideal for group suggestions.
example_one: 一緒に行きましょう！(いっしょにいきましょう！) — Let's go together!
example_two: そろそろ出発しましょうか。(そろそろしゅっぱつしましょうか。) — Shall we get going about now?
supporting_content: ましょうか can also offer your own assistance: 荷物を持ちましょうか。— Shall I carry your bags? In this case it is a one-sided offer, not a joint activity. Compare with ませんか (Lesson 29) which invites the listener.

title: Counting Items in Japanese — Counter Words
content: Japanese attaches counter words to numbers to count specific types of items. The counter changes depending on what is being counted. Common counters: 個 (small objects), 枚 (flat objects), 本 (long cylindrical objects), 冊 (bound books), 匹 (small animals), 台 (machines/vehicles), 杯 (cups or bowls of liquid).
example_one: リンゴを三個ください。(りんごをさんこください。) — Please give me three apples.
example_two: 本を二冊買いました。(ほんをにさつかいました。) — I bought two books.
supporting_content: Numbers often change pronunciation when combined with counters: 一本 (ippon), 二本 (nihon), 三本 (sanbon). The reading shifts based on the counter. Learning the most common counters with their number sound changes is essential.

title: Te Form — Ru-Verbs
content: The te form is a versatile verb form used for connecting actions, making requests, expressing ongoing states, and more. For ru-verbs: drop る and add て. Since the stem of a ru-verb never changes, this is entirely predictable.
example_one: 食べる → 食べて (たべて) — eat (te form)
example_two: 起きる → 起きて (おきて) — wake up (te form)
supporting_content: Te form pattern for ru-verbs: [stem] + て. Examples: 見る→見て, 着る→着て, 出る→出て, 借りる→借りて. All ru-verb te forms are formed the same way.

title: Te Form — U-Verbs (う・つ・る)
content: U-verbs ending in う, つ, or る (u-verb type) form the te form by dropping the final sound and adding って.
example_one: 買う → 買って (かって) — buy (te form)
example_two: 待つ → 待って (まって) — wait (te form)
supporting_content: Pattern: う→って, つ→って, る(u-verb)→って. Ex: 帰る(u-verb) → 帰って (かえって). Be careful not to confuse these with る ru-verbs, which take て instead of って.

title: Te Form — U-Verbs (む・ぶ・ぬ)
content: U-verbs ending in む, ぶ, or ぬ form the te form by dropping the last sound and adding んで.
example_one: 飲む → 飲んで (のんで) — drink (te form)
example_two: 遊ぶ → 遊んで (あそんで) — play (te form)
supporting_content: Pattern: む→んで, ぶ→んで, ぬ→んで. The only common ぬ-ending verb is 死ぬ (しぬ, to die) → 死んで. The んで ending also sounds similar to the number 2 pattern, which learners sometimes notice as a memory aid.

title: Te Form — U-Verbs (く・ぐ・す)
content: U-verbs ending in く form the te form with いて. Verbs ending in ぐ form with いで. Verbs ending in す form with して.
example_one: 書く → 書いて (かいて) — write (te form) / 急ぐ → 急いで (いそいで) — hurry (te form)
example_two: 話す → 話して (はなして) — speak (te form)
supporting_content: Important exception: いく (to go) is irregular — its te form is いって, not いいて. This is one of the few true exceptions among u-verbs and must be memorised.

title: Te Form — Irregular Verbs
content: The two irregular verbs have unpredictable te forms that must be memorised. する (to do) → して. くる (to come) → きて.
example_one: 勉強する → 勉強して (べんきょうして) — study (te form)
example_two: ここに来て。(ここにきて。) — Come here.
supporting_content: Since many compound verbs use する (e.g., 料理する, 運動する, 説明する), knowing して automatically applies to all of them. Similarly all compound verbs using くる follow きて.

title: Polite Requests — ~てください
content: Te form + ください makes a polite request — 'Please do ~'. It asks someone to perform an action. The tone is polite and direct, more like an instruction or request than a question.
example_one: "ここに座ってください。(ここにすわってください。) — Please sit here."
example_two: 名前を書いてください。(なまえをかいてください。) — Please write your name.
supporting_content: To soften it further, add おねがいします after. To make it a question, use てもらえますか or てくださいませんか. てください is standard polite; the alternatives are more indirect.

title: Describing Two Activities Using Te Form
content: The te form connects two or more verbs in sequence — 'do A, and then do B'. The te form verb describes the first action, and the main verb at the end carries the tense for the whole sentence.
example_one: 朝起きて、シャワーを浴びます。(あさおきて、しゃわーをあびます。) — I wake up in the morning and take a shower.
example_two: 図書館に行って、本を借りました。(としょかんにいって、ほんをかりました。) — I went to the library and borrowed a book.
supporting_content: The tense of the whole sentence is carried by the final verb only. Everything before it stays in te form regardless of when it happened. Long chains of te form verbs are common in narrative speech.

title: Granting Permission Using ~てもいいです
content: Te form + もいいです grants permission — 'It is okay to do ~' or 'You may ~'. To ask for permission, convert it into a question: てもいいですか.
example_one: ここに座ってもいいです。(ここにすわってもいいです。) — You may sit here.
example_two: 写真を撮ってもいいですか。(しゃしんをとってもいいですか。) — May I take a photo?
supporting_content: The trio of permission structures is: てもいいです (granting), てはいけません (prohibiting), てもいいですか (requesting permission). Learning all three together is very efficient.

title: Strongly Prohibiting Using ~てはいけません
content: Te form + はいけません strongly prohibits an action — 'You must not ~' or 'It is not allowed to ~'. It is firm in tone and appropriate for rules, warnings, and clear prohibitions.
example_one: ここでタバコを吸ってはいけません。(ここでたばこをすってはいけません。) — You must not smoke here.
example_two: 試験中に話してはいけません。(しけんちゅうにはなしてはいけません。) — You must not speak during the exam.
supporting_content: A softer alternative is ないでください (Lesson 72 — please refrain from). Use てはいけません for strict rules and official notices; use ないでください for polite personal requests.

title: Explaining a Situation Using ~から
content: から connects a reason clause to a result clause — 'because ~, therefore ~'. The reason comes first followed by から, then the result. The politeness level of the verb before から can be either polite or plain form.
example_one: 雨が降っていますから、傘を持ってください。(あめがふっていますから、かさをもってください。) — It is raining, so please take an umbrella.
example_two: お腹が空いたから、何か食べましょう。(おなかがすいたから、なにかたべましょう。) — I'm hungry, so let's eat something.
supporting_content: から can also appear at the very end of a sentence in casual speech to justify what was just said: 疲れているから。— Because I'm tired (explaining a prior statement). This trailing から is very common informally.

title: Offering Assistance Using ~ましょうか
content: Verb stem + ましょうか means 'Shall I ~?' — offering your own assistance to someone else. This differs from ませんか (inviting the listener to join you) and ましょう (suggesting doing something together).
example_one: 荷物を持ちましょうか。(にもつをもちましょうか。) — Shall I carry your bags?
example_two: 窓を開けましょうか。(まどをあけましょうか。) — Shall I open the window?
supporting_content: Compare: 一緒に行きませんか (Won't you come with me? — invitation) vs 案内しましょうか (Shall I show you around? — offering personal help). The key difference is who will perform the action.

title: Actions in Progress — Activity Verb て Form + いる
content: Te form + いる (polite: います) describes an action currently in progress — the equivalent of English '-ing'. The subject is actively engaged in the activity at the moment of speaking.
example_one: 今、本を読んでいます。(いま、ほんをよんでいます。) — I am reading a book right now.
example_two: 田中さんは電話で話しています。(たなかさんはでんわではなしています。) — Mr. Tanaka is talking on the phone.
supporting_content: In casual speech, い is often dropped: 読んでる (yonderu) = 読んでいる. Both are fully acceptable. This informal contraction is extremely common among native speakers.

title: Result of a Change — Change Verb て Form + いる
content: Te form + いる also describes the ongoing result of a completed change — a current state that persists from a past action. The action itself is complete, but its effect continues in the present.
example_one: 電気がついています。(でんきがついています。) — The light is on. (it was turned on and that state persists)
example_two: 結婚しています。(けっこんしています。) — I am married. (the state resulting from having gotten married)
supporting_content: Key distinction: 着る (to put on clothes) is an activity → 着ています can mean currently putting on clothes (in progress) or currently wearing clothes (result). Context usually clarifies which meaning applies.

title: Describing Body Parts — Body Part が + Adjective
content: To describe a physical feature of a person or thing, use the body part as the subject marked with が, followed by an adjective. The person being described is marked with は at the beginning of the sentence.
example_one: 田中さんは目が大きいです。(たなかさんはめがおおきいです。) — Tanaka has big eyes. (literally: Tanaka — eyes are big)
example_two: あの人は髪が長いです。(あのひとはかみがながいです。) — That person has long hair.
supporting_content: The は〜が〜です pattern is fundamental for describing people. It extends to things: この車はエンジンが強いです。— This car has a powerful engine.

title: Creating Compound Sentences with Adjective and Noun Te Forms
content: Adjectives and nouns can be joined using their te forms to list multiple qualities in one sentence. い-adjective te form: drop い and add くて. な-adjective and noun te form: add で after the adjective or noun.
example_one: この部屋は広くてきれいです。(このへやはひろくてきれいです。) — This room is spacious and clean.
example_two: 田中さんは学生で、アルバイトもしています。(たなかさんはがくせいで、あるばいともしています。) — Mr. Tanaka is a student and also works part-time.
supporting_content: い-adjective: 高い → 高くて. な-adjective/noun: 便利 → 便利で, 学生 → 学生で. The te form connection is neutral — it lists qualities without implying cause and effect.

title: Changes from Compounding Japanese Words — れんだく
content: Rendaku (連濁) is a phonological process where the initial consonant of the second element in a compound word becomes voiced. For example: は→ば, た→だ, く→ぐ, す→ず. This occurs in many common compound nouns and is not fully predictable — it must be learned word by word.
example_one: 花 (はな) + 火 (ひ) → 花火 (はなび, fireworks) — ひ becomes び
example_two: 一人 (ひとり, one person) / 二人 (ふたり, two people) — the reading shifts in the compound form
supporting_content: Rendaku is blocked in some compounds for phonological reasons. Exposure through reading and listening is the most reliable way to learn which compounds undergo rendaku. Do not try to apply it systematically.

title: Purpose of Movement — Verb Stem + に行く
content: To express the purpose of going somewhere, use the verb stem (the ます form without ます) plus に plus a movement verb (行く, 来る, 帰る). This means 'to go/come/return in order to do ~'.
example_one: 映画を見に行きます。(えいがをみにいきます。) — I am going to watch a movie.
example_two: 友達に会いに来ました。(ともだちにあいにきました。) — I came to meet my friend.
supporting_content: The noun form of する compound verbs can also be used: 買い物に行きます (かいものにいきます) — I am going shopping. 勉強しに図書館へ行きます — I am going to the library to study.

title: Referring to Family Members
content: Japanese uses different vocabulary for family members depending on whether you are referring to your own family or someone else's. Your own family is referred to with humble terms; other people's family members are referred to with respectful terms.
example_one: 私の母は先生です。(わたしのははせんせいです。) — My mother is a teacher.
example_two: 田中さんのお母さんはどこですか。(たなかさんのおかあさんはどこですか。) — Where is Ms. Tanaka's mother?
supporting_content: Own family: 父(ちち), 母(はは), 兄(あに), 姉(あね), 弟(おとうと), 妹(いもうと). Someone else's: お父さん, お母さん, お兄さん, お姉さん, 弟さん, 妹さん. Using the wrong set can sound impolite.

title: Short Forms — Verbs
content: Verbs have a plain (short) form used in casual speech and as a base for many grammar patterns. Present affirmative: dictionary form. Present negative: verb stem + ない (for ru-verbs) or あ-row change + ない (for u-verbs).
example_one: 食べる → 食べない (casual negative) / 飲む → 飲まない
example_two: する → しない / くる → こない (both irregular)
supporting_content: U-verb negative rule: change う-row to あ-row before ない. 書く→書かない, 飲む→飲まない, 話す→話さない. Special case: う-ending verbs use わ not あ: 買う→買わない.

title: Short Forms — い-Adjectives
content: い-adjectives already have a plain short form — they are used directly without です in casual speech. Present affirmative: the adjective as-is. Present negative: drop い and add くない.
example_one: この映画、面白い。— This movie is interesting. (casual)
example_two: あまり面白くない。— Not very interesting. (casual negative)
supporting_content: Short forms for い-adjectives are identical to the long forms minus です. The negative くない stays the same in both casual and polite speech — polite simply adds です at the end: 面白くないです.

title: Short Forms — な-Adjectives
content: な-adjectives in short form drop です. Present affirmative: adjective alone or followed by the casual copula だ. Present negative: じゃない or ではない.
example_one: この町、静かだ。— This town is quiet. (casual, with だ)
example_two: あそこ、あまりきれいじゃない。— That place is not very pretty. (casual)
supporting_content: だ is the casual copula equivalent of です. In casual speech many speakers — especially women — drop だ entirely in the affirmative: 静か。— Quiet. Both with and without だ are natural.

title: Short Forms — Nouns
content: Nouns follow the same pattern as な-adjectives for short forms. Affirmative present: noun plus だ. Negative present: noun plus じゃない or ではない.
example_one: 彼は学生だ。— He is a student. (casual)
example_two: 彼女は先生じゃない。— She is not a teacher. (casual)
supporting_content: In written Japanese such as diaries, essays, and novels, だ is very common. In spoken conversation the tone of the situation determines whether to include だ or drop it entirely.

title: Using Short Forms as a Sign of Intimacy
content: In casual settings with friends, family, or people of the same or younger age, using short forms signals closeness and informality. Switching from polite form to short form is a mark of trust. In all formal situations, with superiors, or with strangers, always use polite form.
example_one: Polite: 今日、何をしますか。→ Casual: 今日、何する？— What are you doing today?
example_two: Polite: もう食べましたか。→ Casual: もう食べた？— Have you eaten yet?
supporting_content: The shift from polite to casual speech is culturally significant. Using casual form too early can seem rude; staying in polite form too long with close friends can feel distant. Native speakers navigate this naturally through social cues.

title: Saying What You Think — ~と思います
content: To express an opinion or thought, use a short form clause plus と思います — 'I think that ~'. The clause before と always uses the short form (plain form), not the polite form.
example_one: 田中さんは来ると思います。(たなかさんはくるとおもいます。) — I think Tanaka will come.
example_two: この本は難しくないと思います。(このほんはむずかしくないとおもいます。) — I don't think this book is difficult.
supporting_content: To express that you don't think something, negate the verb before と: 〜ないと思います — I think not ~. Avoid saying 〜と思いません, which sounds unnatural in Japanese.

title: Quoted Speech — ~と言っていました
content: To report what someone said, use a short form clause plus と言っていました — 'Someone said that ~'. The content of what was said uses the plain (short) form and is placed before と.
example_one: 田中さんは明日来ると言っていました。(たなかさんはあしたくるといっていました。) — Tanaka said he would come tomorrow.
example_two: 彼女は疲れたと言っていました。(かのじょはつかれたといっていました。) — She said she was tired.
supporting_content: と言いました reports a single past statement precisely. と言っていました carries a slightly more narrative feel, as if relaying something said in passing. Both are acceptable and common.

title: Asking Someone to Refrain — ~ないでください
content: Verb negative short form (ない form) plus でください makes a polite request for someone to refrain from an action — 'Please don't ~'. It is softer in tone than てはいけません.
example_one: ここで写真を撮らないでください。(ここでしゃしんをとらないでください。) — Please don't take photos here.
example_two: 心配しないでください。(しんぱいしないでください。) — Please don't worry.
supporting_content: ないでください is a request (please refrain). てはいけません is a prohibition (you must not). Choose based on context: official rules and signs call for てはいけません; polite personal requests call for ないでください.

title: Turning Verbs into Nouns Using のが
content: Adding の after a short form verb or clause nominalises it — turns the verb phrase into a noun phrase. This noun phrase can then function as the subject (のが), object (のを), or topic (のは) of a sentence. A very common application is のが plus 好き, 嫌い, 上手, or 下手.
example_one: 泳ぐのが好きです。(およぐのがすきです。) — I like swimming.
example_two: 日本語を話すのが難しいです。(にほんごをはなすのがむずかしいです。) — Speaking Japanese is difficult.
supporting_content: の as a nominaliser works similarly to '-ing' in English: 食べるのが好き — liking eating. You will encounter this pattern frequently with ability expressions (のが上手/下手) and preferences.

title: が vs は — Differences Explained
content: Both は and が can mark a subject but carry different nuances. は introduces a topic — usually known information — that the speaker wants to discuss. が introduces new information, answers who or what questions, and is required in specific patterns for likes, abilities, desires, and existence. When in doubt: は = 'speaking of X'; が = 'it is X that'.
example_one: 象は鼻が長い。(ぞうははながながい。) — Elephants have long trunks. (は sets the topic; が marks the feature subject within it)
example_two: 誰が来ましたか。山田さんが来ました。(だれがきましたか。やまださんがきました。) — Who came? Yamada came. (が required in both question and answer)
supporting_content: は can imply contrast or an 'as for X' nuance. が is neutral and marks the subject without additional implication. In yes/no question topics は is typical; in who/what question answers and existence sentences が is required.

title: Something and Anything — なにか・なにも
content: なにか means 'something' and is used in affirmative sentences and questions. なにも plus a negative verb means 'nothing' or 'not anything'. The same pattern applies to people (だれか/だれも) and places (どこか/どこも).
example_one: 何か食べましたか。(なにかたべましたか。) — Did you eat something?
example_two: 何も食べませんでした。(なにもたべませんでした。) — I didn't eat anything.
supporting_content: Pattern: question word plus か = some~ (affirmative). Question word plus も plus negative = no~. Ex: 誰かいますか (Is someone there?) / 誰もいません (Nobody is there).

title: Irregular Verbs Are Usually Just Nouns + する
content: Many compound verbs in Japanese are formed by attaching する to a noun, making them conjugate exactly like the irregular verb する. This is how Japanese generates new verbs from existing nouns and loanwords — almost any noun can become a verb in the right context.
example_one: 勉強する (べんきょうする) — to study / 運動する (うんどうする) — to exercise
example_two: ドライブする (doraibu suru) — to go for a drive / チェックする (chekku suru) — to check
supporting_content: Loanword plus する is especially productive: コピーする (copy), メールする (email), プリントする (print), サインする (sign). This pattern is continuously expanding as new loanwords enter Japanese.

title: Past Tense Short Forms — Verbs
content: Past affirmative short form: for ru-verbs drop る and add た. For u-verbs the past form follows the same sound changes as the te form but uses た or だ instead of て or で. Irregular: した (did), きた (came). Past negative short form: change ない to なかった.
example_one: 食べた (ate), 飲んだ (drank), 書いた (wrote), 話した (spoke)
example_two: 食べなかった (didn't eat), 飲まなかった (didn't drink)
supporting_content: The past short form of verbs parallels the te form precisely: 食べて→食べた, 飲んで→飲んだ, 書いて→書いた. Simply swap て→た and で→だ. This symmetry makes the past short form easy to produce once te form is known.

title: Past Tense Short Forms — い-Adjectives
content: Past affirmative short form: drop い and add かった. Past negative short form: drop い and add くなかった.
example_one: 昨日は寒かった。(きのうはさむかった。) — It was cold yesterday. (casual)
example_two: そんなに高くなかった。(そんなにたかくなかった。) — It wasn't that expensive. (casual)
supporting_content: These are the same forms as polite past but without です at the end: 寒かったです (polite) vs 寒かった (casual). Both the affirmative and negative forms serve as the base for many advanced grammar patterns.

title: Past Tense Short Forms — な-Adjectives
content: Past affirmative short form: adjective plus だった. Past negative short form: adjective plus じゃなかった or ではなかった.
example_one: 子供の頃、ここは静かだった。(こどものころ、ここはしずかだった。) — This place used to be quiet when I was a child.
example_two: 思ったよりきれいじゃなかった。(おもったよりきれいじゃなかった。) — It wasn't as pretty as I expected.
supporting_content: These forms are identical to nouns in the past short form — な-adjectives and nouns share the same conjugation pattern for all tenses in short form.

title: Past Tense Short Forms — Nouns
content: Past affirmative short form: noun plus だった. Past negative short form: noun plus じゃなかった or ではなかった.
example_one: 昔、彼は学生だった。(むかし、かれはがくせいだった。) — He used to be a student.
example_two: それは本当じゃなかった。(それはほんとうじゃなかった。) — That wasn't true.
supporting_content: Noun past short forms: [noun]だった (past affirmative) / [noun]じゃなかった (past negative). The structure is identical to な-adjective past short forms.

title: Past Tense Short Forms Used Informally
content: In casual conversation, past short forms replace the polite past forms ました and でした. With rising intonation or か they form casual past questions. They also serve as the foundation for many advanced grammar patterns in later lessons.
example_one: 昨日、何した？(きのう、なにした？) — What did you do yesterday?
example_two: もう食べた。(もうたべた。) — I already ate.
supporting_content: Short past forms are indispensable for natural casual conversation and are the building block for patterns such as たことがある (Lesson 101), the たら conditional, and たり〜たりする (Lesson 100).

title: Saying What You Think Happened — ~と思います (Past)
content: To express an opinion about something in the past, use the past short form plus と思います. The clause uses the past tense to reflect that the event already occurred, while と思います stays present because it expresses your current belief about the past.
example_one: 田中さんはもう帰ったと思います。(たなかさんはもうかえったとおもいます。) — I think Tanaka has already gone home.
example_two: それは間違いだったと思います。(それはまちがいだったとおもいます。) — I think that was a mistake.
supporting_content: The clause before と uses whatever tense matches its meaning. と思います itself always stays in the present because it describes your current state of thinking.

title: Quoting What Someone Said — ~と言っていました
content: When reporting what someone said using plain form quotes, the quoted content uses the appropriate short form tense to match what was actually communicated, while と言っていました frames it as reported speech.
example_one: 彼は仕事が大変だと言っていました。(かれはしごとがたいへんだといっていました。) — He said his job was tough.
example_two: 先生は明日テストがあると言っていました。(せんせいはあしたてすとがあるといっていました。) — The teacher said there would be a test tomorrow.
supporting_content: Direct quote: 「明日来ます」と言いました — Said 'I will come tomorrow'. Indirect quote: 明日来ると言っていました — Said that they would come tomorrow. Short form is standard in indirect quotes.

title: Describing Nouns With Adjectives
content: Adjectives in their short form — including past and negative forms — can directly precede a noun to modify it. This allows complex noun phrases such as 'a room that is not expensive' or 'a movie that was boring'.
example_one: 高くない店 (たかくないみせ) — a store that is not expensive
example_two: 有名な人 (ゆうめいなひと) — a famous person / 有名じゃない人 (ゆうめいじゃないひと) — a person who is not famous
supporting_content: い-adj modifying a noun: plain form in any tense + noun. な-adj modifying a noun: [adj]な + noun (affirmative) / [adj]じゃない + noun (negative) / [adj]だった + noun (past).

title: Describing Nouns With Adjectives and Verbs
content: Verbs in their short form can also precede a noun to create relative clauses — the equivalent of 'who', 'that', or 'which' in English. Japanese places the entire modifying clause before the noun with no separate relative pronoun.
example_one: 昨日読んだ本 (きのうよんだほん) — the book that I read yesterday
example_two: 日本語を話す人 (にほんごをはなすひと) — a person who speaks Japanese
supporting_content: Any verb in any short form tense can precede a noun: 食べる人 (person who eats), 食べた人 (person who ate), 食べない人 (person who doesn't eat), 食べなかった人 (person who didn't eat). This pattern is one of the most powerful in Japanese.

title: Saying an Event Happened — ~ました・もう~ました
content: ました states that an action occurred in the past. Adding もう (already) before the verb adds the nuance that the action is already completed — often used in response to an expectation or a question.
example_one: 宿題をしました。(しゅくだいをしました。) — I did my homework.
example_two: もう食べました。(もうたべました。) — I have already eaten.
supporting_content: もう in the affirmative means 'already' (completed). もう in the negative — まだ〜ていません (Lesson 87) — means 'not yet'. These two naturally pair together in conversation about completed versus pending actions.

title: Saying an Event Has Not Happened — ~ていません・まだ~ていません
content: ていません states that an action has not been done or is not currently in progress. まだ (yet) plus ていません emphasises that the action has not happened yet — often implying it was expected to have occurred by now.
example_one: 宿題はまだしていません。(しゅくだいはまだしていません。) — I haven't done my homework yet.
example_two: 映画はもう見ましたか。いいえ、まだ見ていません。— Have you already seen the movie? No, not yet.
supporting_content: もう〜ましたか (have you already ~?) pairs naturally with まだ〜ていません (not yet). Together they form the backbone of conversations about whether tasks or events have been completed.

title: Explaining a Situation Using ~から in One Sentence
content: から connects a reason and its result within a single sentence. The reason clause comes first in plain or polite form followed by から, then the result. The formality of から adapts to the surrounding speech level.
example_one: 眠いから、早く寝ます。(ねむいから、はやくねます。) — Since I'm sleepy, I'll sleep early.
example_two: 雨が降りそうだから、傘を持っていこう。(あめがふりそうだから、かさをもっていこう。) — Since it looks like rain, let's bring an umbrella.
supporting_content: Compare から (more subjective, casual reason — personal justification or excuse) with ので (Lesson 105, more objective, formal reason — a logical or situational given). Choose based on register and the nature of the explanation.

title: Comparing Two Items — ~のほうが・~より
content: To compare two items, use A のほうが B より [adjective] — 'A is more [adjective] than B'. のほうが marks the item with more of the quality; より marks the item it is being compared against.
example_one: 猫のほうが犬より静かです。(ねこのほうがいぬよりしずかです。) — Cats are quieter than dogs.
example_two: 電車のほうがバスより速いです。(でんしゃのほうがばすよりはやいです。) — Trains are faster than buses.
supporting_content: より can appear separately for emphasis: 電車のほうが速い。バスより。— Trains are faster. Compared to buses. Japanese adjectives do not change form in comparisons — no more or most equivalent is needed.

title: Asking to Compare Two Items — どちらのほうが・どっちのほうが
content: To ask which of two items has more of a quality, use どちらのほうが (formal) or どっちのほうが (casual) plus an adjective plus ですか, with the two items listed using と.
example_one: 犬と猫とどちらのほうが好きですか。(いぬとねことどちらのほうがすきですか。) — Which do you like better, dogs or cats?
example_two: 東京と大阪とどっちのほうが大きいですか。(とうきょうとおおさかとどっちのほうがおおきいですか。) — Which is bigger, Tokyo or Osaka?
supporting_content: どちら is the formal/neutral form; どっち is casual. Both ask for a choice between exactly two options. For three or more items use the superlative pattern in Lesson 91.

title: Comparison Among Three or More Items — ~のなかで~いちばん
content: To express a superlative among a group of three or more, use [group] のなかで [item] がいちばん [adjective] — '[item] is the most [adjective] among [group]'.
example_one: 果物のなかでりんごがいちばん好きです。(くだもののなかでりんごがいちばんすきです。) — Among fruits, I like apples the most.
example_two: このクラスのなかで田中さんがいちばん背が高い。(このくらすのなかでたなかさんがいちばんせがたかい。) — Among this class, Tanaka is the tallest.
supporting_content: To ask the superlative: [group]のなかでなにがいちばん〜ですか。— What is the most ~ among [group]? This naturally extends the two-item comparison from Lesson 89.

title: Omitting Noun in Adjective + Noun Using の
content: When the noun being modified is clear from context, it can be replaced by の — turning an adjective plus noun phrase into adjective plus の. This is similar to 'the [adjective] one' in English.
example_one: 赤いのをください。(あかいのをください。) — Please give me the red one.
example_two: 大きいのと小さいのとどちらがいいですか。(おおきいのとちいさいのとどちらがいいですか。) — Which would you like, the big one or the small one?
supporting_content: の here substitutes for a known noun. It can follow い-adjectives directly, な-adjectives with な (きれいなの), and even nouns to show possession: 田中さんのは — Tanaka's one.

title: Saying What You Are Planning to Do — ~つもり
content: Short form present verb plus つもりです expresses the speaker's intention or plan — 'I intend to ~' or 'I plan to ~'. It conveys a fairly firm intention rather than a vague hope.
example_one: 来年、日本に行くつもりです。(らいねん、にほんにいくつもりです。) — I intend to go to Japan next year.
example_two: 今日は家でゆっくりするつもりです。(きょうはいえでゆっくりするつもりです。) — I plan to relax at home today.
supporting_content: Negative intention: ないつもりです — I don't plan to. 今日は勉強しないつもりです。— I don't plan to study today. Past form: つもりでした — I had intended to (but circumstances changed).

title: Indicating a Change — Adjective + なる
content: なる (to become) follows adjective forms to express a change of state. い-adjectives: drop い and add くなる. な-adjectives and nouns: add になる.
example_one: 日本語が上手になりました。(にほんごがじょうずになりました。) — My Japanese has gotten better.
example_two: 最近、寒くなってきました。(さいきん、さむくなってきました。) — It has been getting cold recently.
supporting_content: Common combinations: 上手になる (to get better at), 好きになる (to come to like), 大人になる (to become an adult), 便利になる (to become convenient). All use the same pattern.

title: Saying 'Someone' and 'Not Anyone'
content: だれか means 'someone' and appears in affirmative statements and questions. だれも plus a negative verb means 'nobody' or 'not anyone'. This mirrors the なにか/なにも pattern from Lesson 75.
example_one: 誰かが来ました。(だれかがきました。) — Someone came.
example_two: 誰も来ませんでした。(だれもきませんでした。) — Nobody came.
supporting_content: Full indefinite pronoun set: 誰か (someone) / 誰も+neg (nobody) / 何か (something) / 何も+neg (nothing) / どこか (somewhere) / どこも+neg (nowhere) / どれか (one of them) / どれも+neg (none of them).

title: Saying 'Somewhere' and 'Not Anywhere'
content: どこか means 'somewhere' and appears in affirmative statements and questions. どこも plus a negative verb means 'nowhere' or 'not anywhere'.
example_one: 週末にどこかへ行きましたか。(しゅうまつにどこかへいきましたか。) — Did you go somewhere on the weekend?
example_two: 今日はどこにも行きません。(きょうはどこにもいきません。) — I'm not going anywhere today.
supporting_content: どこにも and どこへも both work with negative movement verbs. どこにも plus いません = not anywhere (existence). どこへも plus 行きません = not going anywhere (movement direction).

title: Using で for Means of Transportation
content: で marks the vehicle or mode of transportation used for an action — 'by/on [transportation]'. This extends the particle's core meaning of 'by means of' to the domain of travel.
example_one: 電車で東京に行きます。(でんしゃでとうきょうにいきます。) — I go to Tokyo by train.
example_two: バスで学校に来ました。(ばすでがっこうにきました。) — I came to school by bus.
supporting_content: Common forms: 電車で (by train), バスで (by bus), タクシーで (by taxi), 飛行機で (by plane), 自転車で (by bicycle). Exception: 歩いて (aruite — by walking) uses the te form of 歩く rather than で.

title: Using で for Tools and Means
content: で also marks the tool, instrument, or language used to perform an action. This is the same particle as Place of Action (Lesson 25) and Transportation (Lesson 97) — all share the core meaning of 'using' or 'by means of'.
example_one: 箸で食べます。(はしでたべます。) — I eat with chopsticks.
example_two: 日本語で話しましょう。(にほんごではなしましょう。) — Let's speak in Japanese.
supporting_content: で as means: 鉛筆で書く (write with a pencil), 電話で話す (talk by phone), 日本語で説明する (explain in Japanese), インターネットで調べる (look up on the internet).

title: Describing a Hope or Aspiration — ~たい
content: Verb stem plus たい expresses the speaker's desire or wish to do something — 'I want to ~'. たい conjugates like an い-adjective: present negative is たくない, past is たかった, past negative is たくなかった.
example_one: 日本に行きたいです。(にほんにいきたいです。) — I want to go to Japan.
example_two: もっと日本語が話せるようになりたい。(もっとにほんごがはなせるようになりたい。) — I want to get to where I can speak more Japanese.
supporting_content: たい is for the speaker's own desires only. For describing what someone else wants, use たがっている: 彼は行きたがっています — He seems to want to go (he is showing signs of wanting to go).

title: Mentioning Things in No Set Order — ~たり~たりする
content: Using the past short form plus り for multiple actions and ending with する (in the appropriate tense) lists activities as representative examples from a larger set — 'doing things like A and B'. The actions are not sequential and the list is not exhaustive.
example_one: 週末は映画を見たり、友達と話したりします。(しゅうまつはえいがをみたり、ともだちとはなしたりします。) — On weekends I do things like watching movies and talking with friends.
example_two: 泣いたり笑ったりしました。(ないたりわらったりしました。) — I was going back and forth between crying and laughing.
supporting_content: Pattern: [verb past short]り〜[verb past short]りする. The final する carries the tense for the whole sentence. A minimum of two verbs is standard, though three or more is also common.

title: Mentioning Something Done in the Past — ~ことがある
content: Past short form plus ことがある (koto ga aru) expresses that one has had the experience of doing something at some point in one's life — 'have ever done ~'. The negative ことがない means 'have never done ~'.
example_one: 富士山に登ったことがあります。(ふじさんにのぼったことがあります。) — I have climbed Mt. Fuji before.
example_two: 刺身を食べたことがありません。(さしみをたべたことがありません。) — I have never eaten sashimi.
supporting_content: ことがある describes lifetime experience, not a specific recent past event. Compare: 食べました (I ate it, simple past) vs 食べたことがあります (I have eaten it at some point in my life, as an experience).

title: Proposing Examples Using や as a Particle
content: や lists nouns as examples from a larger group — 'A and B and so on' — implying the list is not exhaustive. This contrasts with と, which lists all items completely. や is used when you want to suggest a representative sample without naming everything.
example_one: 机の上に本やペンがあります。(つくえのうえにほんやぺんがあります。) — On the desk there are things like books and pens.
example_two: 果物や野菜を買いました。(くだもののやさいをかいました。) — I bought things like fruit and vegetables.
supporting_content: と = complete list (A and B, that is all). や = partial list (A and B, among others). など (nado — et cetera) is often added after a や list to reinforce the open-ended nature: 本やペンなど。

title: Helping Verb for Excess — ~すぎる
content: すぎる attached to a verb stem or adjective means 'to do too much of ~' or 'to be too ~'. For verbs: verb stem plus すぎる. For い-adjectives: drop い and add すぎる. For な-adjectives: adjective plus すぎる.
example_one: 食べすぎました。(たべすぎました。) — I ate too much.
example_two: この荷物は重すぎて持てません。(このにもつはおもすぎてもてません。) — This luggage is too heavy to carry.
supporting_content: すぎる conjugates as a ru-verb: すぎます (polite), すぎた (past), すぎて (te form). Example with te form: 飲みすぎて気分が悪いです — I drank too much and feel sick.

title: Giving Advice — ~ほうがいいです
content: To give advice, use the past short form plus ほうがいいです — 'You should ~'. To advise against something, use the present negative short form plus ほうがいいです — 'You'd better not ~'.
example_one: 早く寝たほうがいいですよ。(はやくねたほうがいいですよ。) — You should sleep early.
example_two: 無理しないほうがいいです。(むりしないほうがいいです。) — You'd better not push yourself too hard.
supporting_content: Past form plus ほうがいいです = should do (advice to act). Negative present plus ほうがいいです = should not do. The past form here does not indicate past tense — it is a fixed pattern for giving advice.

title: Explaining a Situation Formally — ~ので
content: ので is similar to から but more formal and objective. It presents a reason as a natural or logical consequence rather than a personal justification. Short form plus ので connects a cause to its result. ので is preferred in formal writing, professional speech, and polite explanations.
example_one: 明日は試験があるので、今日は早く帰ります。(あしたはしけんがあるので、きょうははやくかえります。) — Since there is an exam tomorrow, I am going home early today.
example_two: 電車が遅れたので、遅刻しました。(でんしゃがおくれたので、ちこくしました。) — Because the train was delayed, I was late.
supporting_content: から vs ので: から is more subjective and casual — often a personal reason or excuse. ので is more objective and formal — used when the reason is a logical given. In polite and professional contexts, prefer ので.

title: Saying You Must Do Something — ~なければいけません (Formal)
content: Verb negative short form (ない form, remove い) plus ければいけません expresses obligation — 'must do ~' or 'have to do ~'. Literally interpreted: 'if I do not do X, it will not do'.
example_one: 宿題をしなければいけません。(しゅくだいをしなければいけません。) — I have to do my homework.
example_two: もっと練習しなければいけません。(もっとれんしゅうしなければいけません。) — I have to practice more.
supporting_content: Formation: verb ない form → remove い → add ければいけません. Example: 食べない → 食べなければいけません. A fully formal alternative ending: なければなりません, used in official or very stiff registers.

title: Saying You Must Do Something — ~なきゃいけません (Colloquial)
content: なきゃいけません is a colloquial contraction of なければいけません with the same meaning — 'have to' or 'must'. In very casual speech it is shortened further to just なきゃ or なくちゃ.
example_one: 早く行かなきゃいけない！(はやくいかなきゃいけない！) — I have to go quickly!
example_two: 勉強しなきゃ。(べんきょうしなきゃ。) — I gotta study.
supporting_content: Casual shortening: なければいけません → なきゃいけません → なきゃ (most casual). All mean must or have to. Use なければいけません in formal contexts and なきゃ with close friends.

title: Asking a Question Politely — ~でしょうか
content: でしょうか replaces ですか to make a question more formal and polite. It is used in business settings, formal situations, and when addressing strangers or superiors. It softens the directness of a question and signals a high degree of respect.
example_one: よろしいでしょうか。(よろしいでしょうか。) — Is that alright? / Would that be okay?
example_two: ご出席いただけるでしょうか。(ごしゅっせきいただけるでしょうか。) — Would you be able to attend?
supporting_content: でしょうか also expresses uncertainty or conjecture: 明日は晴れるでしょうか。— I wonder if it will be sunny tomorrow. In this use it can be rhetorical — not expecting a direct answer.

title: Trying Something Out — ~てみる
content: Attach てみる to the て-form of a verb to express trying something to see what it is like. The focus is on attempting an action out of curiosity or to test a result. It does not imply the action is difficult — only that you are giving it a go.
example_one: この料理を食べてみてください。(このりょうりをたべてみてください。) — Please try eating this dish.
example_two: 一度日本に行ってみたいです。(いちどにほんにいってみたいです。) — I want to try going to Japan once.
supporting_content: てみる conjugates like a regular る-verb: present てみます, past てみました, negative てみません. It is frequently combined with たい (want) to form てみたい — want to try doing.

title: Finishing / Regretting — ~てしまう
content: て-form plus しまう has two related meanings: (1) completing an action fully and finally — going ahead and finishing; (2) doing something regrettable or unintentional — ending up doing something by accident. Context determines which reading applies.
example_one: 宿題をやってしまいました。(しゅくだいをやってしまいました。) — I finished my homework completely.
example_two: 財布を忘れてしまいました。(さいふをわすれてしまいました。) — I accidentally left my wallet behind.
supporting_content: Colloquial contractions: てしまう → ちゃう, でしまう → じゃう. Example: 食べてしまった → 食べちゃった. These contractions often signal regret or surprise in casual speech.

title: Change Coming Toward Now — ~てくる
content: て-form plus くる expresses a change or action that began in the past and continues toward the present, or movement toward the speaker. It can indicate: (1) a gradual change up to now; (2) going to do something and coming back; (3) a state that developed over time.
example_one: 日本語が少し上手になってきました。(にほんごがすこしじょうずになってきました。) — My Japanese has been getting a little better.
example_two: コーヒーを買ってきます。(コーヒーをかってきます。) — I will go buy coffee and come back.
supporting_content: Compare てくる (toward now/speaker) vs ていく (away from now/speaker). 暗くなってきた — it has been getting dark (up to now). 暗くなっていく — it is going to get darker (from now on).

title: Change Moving Away — ~ていく
content: て-form plus いく expresses a change that starts now and continues into the future, or movement away from the speaker. It indicates: (1) a gradual change from now onward; (2) going somewhere to do something and not necessarily returning; (3) a state developing away from the current point.
example_one: これから寒くなっていくでしょう。(これからさむくなっていくでしょう。) — It will probably keep getting colder from now on.
example_two: お弁当を持っていきます。(おべんとうをもっていきます。) — I will take my lunch box with me.
supporting_content: てくる focuses on change arriving at the present; ていく focuses on change departing into the future. Both can be used with movement verbs literally: 走ってきた — came running; 走っていった — went running away.

title: Giving and Receiving — あげる / もらう / くれる
content: Japanese has three core giving-and-receiving verbs that depend on who gives and who receives. あげる: the subject gives to someone else. もらう: the subject receives from someone. くれる: someone gives to me or my in-group. The choice of verb encodes the direction of the exchange from the speaker's perspective.
example_one: 友達にプレゼントをあげました。(ともだちにプレゼントをあげました。) — I gave a present to my friend.
example_two: 先生に本をもらいました。(せんせいにほんをもらいました。) — I received a book from the teacher.
supporting_content: くれる example: 田中さんがケーキをくれました。— Mr Tanaka gave me a cake. The speaker is the recipient with くれる. Use に for the receiver with あげる, and に or から for the giver with もらう.

title: Doing Favours — ~てあげる / ~てもらう / ~てくれる
content: Attach giving-and-receiving verbs to the て-form to express doing or having done a favour. ~てあげる: I do something for someone else. ~てもらう: I have someone do something for me. ~てくれる: someone does something for me as a favour.
example_one: 荷物を運んであげました。(にもつをはこんであげました。) — I carried the luggage for her.
example_two: 先生に説明してもらいました。(せんせいにせつめいしてもらいました。) — I had the teacher explain it to me.
supporting_content: くれる example: 友達が手伝ってくれた — my friend helped me as a favour. When てあげる sounds condescending, use てくれませんか instead. てもらえますか is a polite way to ask someone to do something for you.

title: Before Doing Something — ~前に
content: Verb dictionary form plus 前に (まえに) means before doing something. Unlike て-form sequences, the verb before 前に always uses the dictionary form regardless of when the action occurs — it does not change to match the tense of the main clause.
example_one: 寝る前に歯を磨きます。(ねるまえにはをみがきます。) — I brush my teeth before going to sleep.
example_two: 旅行する前に地図を確認しました。(りょこうするまえにちずをかくにんしました。) — I checked the map before travelling.
supporting_content: Contrast: verb-た + 後で (after doing). The verb before 前に is always in dictionary form even in past-tense sentences: 食べる前に手を洗った — I washed my hands before eating. Noun + の前に is also possible: 授業の前に — before class.

title: After Doing — ~てから
content: て-form plus から means after doing something, then something else. It expresses a sequence where the first action must be completed before the second begins. てから emphasises the order of events more explicitly than just using the て-form alone.
example_one: シャワーを浴びてから、朝ごはんを食べます。(シャワーをあびてから、あさごはんをたべます。) — After taking a shower, I eat breakfast.
example_two: 考えてから返事します。(かんがえてからへんじします。) — I will reply after thinking about it.
supporting_content: Compare with 後で (after / later): てから emphasises sequential order (A, then B); 後で is more loosely at some later point. また後で連絡します — I will contact you later. 食べてから連絡します — I will contact you after eating.

title: After / Later — ~後で
content: Verb past short form plus 後で (あとで) means after doing something or later. Noun plus の後で means after a noun. 後で on its own means later without specifying what comes before. The nuance is looser than てから — it does not strongly imply an unbroken sequence.
example_one: 食べた後で、散歩しましょう。(たべたあとで、さんぽしましょう。) — Let us take a walk after eating.
example_two: 後で電話します。(あとででんわします。) — I will call later.
supporting_content: 後で vs てから: 食べてから行く — go right after eating (ordered sequence). 食べた後で行く — go after eating, possibly with a gap. 授業の後で — after class (noun form). 後ほど is a more formal equivalent used in business.

title: When / At the Time — ~とき
content: とき (時) attached to a verb or adjective means when or at the time of. The tense of the verb before とき matters: dictionary form before とき means the main action happens during the described state; past form before とき means the main action happens after the described action is completed.
example_one: 子どものとき、よく泳ぎました。(こどものとき、よくおよぎました。) — When I was a child, I often swam.
example_two: 日本に来るとき、辞書を買いました。(にほんにくるとき、じしょをかいました。) — When coming to Japan, I bought a dictionary (before arriving).
supporting_content: Tense contrast: 日本に来たとき — when I arrived in Japan (already there). 日本に来るとき — before arriving in Japan. Noun + の + とき: 雨のとき — when it rains. い-adj plain form + とき: 暑いとき — when it is hot.

title: Doing Two Things Simultaneously — ~ながら
content: Verb masu-stem plus ながら expresses doing two actions at the same time. The main focus is the second (main) verb; ながら describes a simultaneous background action. Both actions must be performed by the same subject.
example_one: 音楽を聴きながら、勉強します。(おんがくをききながら、べんきょうします。) — I study while listening to music.
example_two: 歩きながら電話しないでください。(あるきながらでんわしないでください。) — Please do not talk on the phone while walking.
supporting_content: Formation: masu-stem + ながら (e.g., 飲みます → 飲みながら). The main activity comes after ながら. Both actions share the same subject — you cannot use ながら when two different people do different actions. For different subjects, use 〜ているとき or 〜ているあいだ.

title: The たら Conditional — ~たら
content: Verb past short form plus ら forms the たら conditional. It means if or when something happens. たら is the most versatile conditional: it covers hypothetical situations, sequential events, and completed-action conditions. It often implies that the first action is completed before the second occurs.
example_one: 雨が降ったら、出かけません。(あめがふったら、でかけません。) — If it rains, I will not go out.
example_two: 家に帰ったら、電話してください。(いえにかえったら、でんわしてください。) — When you get home, please call me.
supporting_content: Formation: た-form + ら. い-adj: 安かったら (if cheap). な-adj/noun: 暇だったら (if free). たら is used for a one-time future event or a completed hypothetical. Compare: と (natural consequence, habitual), ば (hypothetical), なら (conditional on new information).

title: The ば Conditional — ~ば
content: The ば conditional expresses if A then B with an emphasis on a hypothetical or ideal condition. It often appears in proverbs, general truths, and situations where if the condition is met, the result naturally follows.
example_one: 春になれば、花が咲きます。(はるになれば、はながさきます。) — If spring comes, the flowers will bloom.
example_two: もっと練習すれば、上手になりますよ。(もっとれんしゅうすれば、じょうずになりますよ。) — If you practice more, you will improve.
supporting_content: Formation for u-verbs: change final う to えば row (書く→書けば, 飲む→飲めば). Ru-verbs: remove る + れば (食べる→食べれば). する→すれば, くる→くれば. い-adj: remove い + ければ (高い→高ければ). ば is not used when the main clause is a command or strong volition directed at the speaker.

title: The と Conditional — ~と
content: Verb or adjective in plain form plus と expresses an automatic or natural consequence — if or when A, B always results. It is used for natural laws, habitual facts, directions, and instructions. It cannot be used when the main clause contains a command, request, or volitional expression.
example_one: 春になると、桜が咲きます。(はるになると、さくらがさきます。) — When spring comes, the cherry blossoms bloom.
example_two: この道をまっすぐ行くと、駅に着きます。(このみちをまっすぐいくと、えきにつきます。) — If you go straight down this road, you will reach the station.
supporting_content: と is used when A reliably leads to B — not for one-off future decisions. The main clause cannot end in a volition, command, or request. Use たら or ば for those situations. Compare: 春になったら花が咲く — if spring comes (one-time event).

title: The なら Conditional — ~なら
content: なら follows a plain form noun, verb, or adjective and means if that is the case or if it is true that. It is typically triggered by information the speaker has just received or assumed — responding to a premise. It is not about physical consequences but about taking a position given new information.
example_one: 日本に行くなら、京都がおすすめです。(にほんにいくなら、きょうとがおすすめです。) — If you are going to Japan, I recommend Kyoto.
example_two: 病気なら、早く帰ってください。(びょうきなら、はやくかえってください。) — If you are sick, please go home early.
supporting_content: なら uniquely allows the speaker to make a recommendation based on an assumed premise. Unlike と or たら, the なら clause does not need to occur before the main clause chronologically. Often used in advice-giving: 魚なら新鮮なうちに食べて — if it is fish, eat it while fresh.

title: Expressing Possibility — ~かもしれない
content: Plain form plus かもしれない (formal: かもしれません) expresses possibility or uncertainty — might / may / possibly. The speaker is genuinely unsure and is hedging their statement. It indicates a real possibility without strong confidence either way.
example_one: 明日は雨かもしれません。(あしたはあめかもしれません。) — It might rain tomorrow.
example_two: 彼はもう知っているかもしれない。(かれはもうしっているかもしれない。) — He might already know.
supporting_content: Attach to verb/い-adj plain form, or na-adj/noun + かもしれない (without だ). Example: 静かかもしれない (not 静かだかもしれない). Compared to でしょう (leaning toward certainty) and はずだ (logical expectation), かもしれない sits at the uncertain end of the spectrum.

title: Expressing Probability — ~でしょう
content: でしょう (casual: だろう) follows the plain form of a verb, adjective, or noun and expresses a fairly confident conjecture or prediction — probably / I think / it seems likely. The speaker bases their assessment on available evidence or general knowledge.
example_one: 明日は晴れるでしょう。(あしたははれるでしょう。) — It will probably be sunny tomorrow.
example_two: 彼女は今、忙しいでしょう。(かのじょはいま、いそがしいでしょう。) — She is probably busy right now.
supporting_content: でしょう is stronger than かもしれない (may) but weaker than はずだ (should logically be). Rising intonation on でしょう? turns it into a confirmation question — right? / is that not so? Casual form だろう is used by men in informal speech.

title: Expressing Expectation — ~はずだ
content: はず is a noun meaning expectation or supposed outcome. Plain form plus はずだ (formal: はずです) states that something should logically be the case based on knowledge or reasoning. It conveys that the speaker is confident based on evidence or prior information.
example_one: 彼は今日来るはずです。(かれはきょうくるはずです。) — He should be coming today (I know he planned to).
example_two: この薬を飲めば、よくなるはずです。(このくすりをのめば、よくなるはずです。) — If you take this medicine, you should get better.
supporting_content: はずがない is the negative form — there is no way / cannot possibly be: 彼が嘘をつくはずがない — there is no way he would lie. Unlike でしょう (general guess), はずだ is based on specific reasoning. If the expected outcome is wrong, use はずだったのに — it was supposed to be, but...

title: Seeking or Giving Explanations — ~んです / ~のです
content: Plain form plus んです (spoken) or のです (written/formal) signals that the speaker is explaining, seeking an explanation, or emphasising context. It adds the nuance of the thing is or that is why. Compared to a plain statement, んです invites or provides a reason and connects more personally to the listener.
example_one: どうして遅刻したんですか。(どうしておくれたんですか。) — Why were you late? (seeking an explanation)
example_two: 電車が止まったんです。(でんしゃがとまったんです。) — The thing is, the train stopped. (explaining)
supporting_content: Formation: verb/い-adj plain form + んです. For な-adj and nouns, use な + んです: 病気なんです — the thing is, I am sick. Common in questions: どうしたんですか — what happened? Common in explanations: ちょっと急いでいるんです — the thing is, I am in a bit of a hurry.

title: Purpose and Cause — ~ために
content: ために (為に) follows the dictionary form of a verb or a noun plus の to express purpose — in order to / for the sake of. It can also follow a plain form to express cause — because of / due to. Context distinguishes purpose from cause.
example_one: 日本語を勉強するために、毎日練習します。(にほんごをべんきょうするために、まいにちれんしゅうします。) — In order to study Japanese, I practice every day.
example_two: 事故のために、電車が遅れました。(じこのために、でんしゃがおくれました。) — Due to the accident, the train was delayed.
supporting_content: Purpose ために: subject of both clauses is typically the same; verb before ために is in dictionary form. Cause ために: follows a completed or factual situation. Compare ために (purpose/cause) with ように (goal for a gradual change or state): 聞こえるように大きく話す — speak loudly so that I can be heard.

title: Goals and Soft Purpose — ~ように
content: Dictionary form or negative form plus ように expresses a goal or purpose where the outcome is a state rather than a direct action — so that / in order to / hoping that. Unlike ために (direct purpose), ように often indicates effort toward a gradual or hoped-for result.
example_one: 聞こえるように、大きい声で話してください。(きこえるように、おおきいこえではなしてください。) — Please speak loudly so that I can hear you.
example_two: 遅刻しないように、早く起きました。(ちこくしないように、はやくおきました。) — I woke up early so as not to be late.
