supporting_content: ように vs ために: ために + volitional action verb (走るために — in order to run). ように + potential/stative/negative verb (走れるように — so that I can run; 忘れないように — so as not to forget). ように also appears in requests to a third party: 先生に来るように伝えてください — please tell the teacher to come.

title: Coming to a New State — ~ようになる
content: Dictionary or negative plain form plus ようになる expresses a gradual change to a new ability, habit, or state — came to / has gotten to the point of / started to. It emphasises a transition rather than a sudden switch.
example_one: 日本語が話せるようになりました。(にほんごがはなせるようになりました。) — I have come to be able to speak Japanese.
example_two: 毎日野菜を食べるようになりました。(まいにちやさいをたべるようになりました。) — I have come to eat vegetables every day.
supporting_content: Contrast with ようにする (making an effort): ようになる = a change that has happened (result). ようにする = deliberately trying to make something happen (effort). Negative form: 肉を食べないようになった — I have stopped eating meat. Very common for describing language learning progress.

title: Making an Effort — ~ようにする
content: Dictionary or negative plain form plus ようにする expresses deliberate effort to do or not do something — I try to / I make it a point to / I make sure to. The speaker is actively working toward a behaviour or habit.
example_one: 毎日30分歩くようにしています。(まいにち30ふんあるくようにしています。) — I try to walk for 30 minutes every day.
example_two: 夜遅く食べないようにしています。(よるおそくたべないようにしています。) — I make sure not to eat late at night.
supporting_content: ようにする pairs naturally with ている to express an ongoing rule: ようにしています — I make it a rule to. Compare: ようになる (a change has occurred) vs ようにする (making a conscious effort). Requests to others: もっと早く来るようにしてください — please try to come earlier.

title: Easy or Hard to Do — ~やすい / ~にくい
content: Verb masu-stem plus やすい means easy to do or tends to happen easily. Verb masu-stem plus にくい means hard to do or difficult. Both conjugate like い-adjectives and describe inherent qualities of actions or objects.
example_one: この本は読みやすいです。(このほんはよみやすいです。) — This book is easy to read.
example_two: この薬は飲みにくいです。(このくすりはのみにくいです。) — This medicine is hard to swallow.
supporting_content: Formation: masu-stem + やすい/にくい. 書きやすいペン — a pen that is easy to write with. 分かりにくい説明 — a difficult-to-understand explanation. Compare: にくい (hard to do physically) vs づらい (difficult in an emotional or psychological sense): 話しにくい vs 話しづらい.

title: Looks Like / Seems Like — ~そうだ (appearance)
content: Verb masu-stem or adjective stem plus そうだ expresses the speaker's judgment based on direct visual or sensory evidence — looks like / seems like / appears to be. This そうだ is based on what the speaker directly observes in the moment.
example_one: 雨が降りそうです。(あめがふりそうです。) — It looks like it is going to rain.
example_two: このケーキはおいしそうです。(このケーキはおいしそうです。) — This cake looks delicious.
supporting_content: Formation: verb masu-stem + そう (降りそう); い-adj drop い + そう (おいしい→おいしそう); な-adj drop な + そう (元気→元気そう). Special cases: いい → よさそう; ない → なさそう. This pattern is NOT used with nouns. Contrast with hearsay そうだ which attaches to plain form complete sentences.

title: I Heard That — ~そうだ (hearsay)
content: Plain form sentence plus そうだ (formal: そうです) expresses information received from an external source — I heard that / they say that / apparently. This そうだ attaches to a complete sentence in plain form, signalling that the content is secondhand information.
example_one: 明日は雪が降るそうです。(あしたはゆきがふるそうです。) — I heard it will snow tomorrow.
example_two: 田中さんは来月結婚するそうです。(たなかさんはらいげつけっこんするそうです。) — I heard that Mr Tanaka is getting married next month.
supporting_content: Hearsay そうだ attaches to the end of a full sentence (降るそうだ / 元気だそうだ / 休みだそうだ). Contrast with appearance そうだ which attaches to verb masu-stem or adj stem. 雨が降りそうだ — looks like rain (visual). 雨が降るそうだ — I heard it will rain (hearsay).

title: Apparently / Seems Like — ~らしい
content: Plain form plus らしい expresses an inference the speaker draws from indirect evidence, rumours, or general impression — apparently / it seems / it appears. Unlike hearsay そうだ (pure reported speech) and ようだ (direct observation), らしい blends secondhand information with the speaker's own inference.
example_one: 彼は病気らしいです。(かれはびょうきらしいです。) — He apparently is sick / it seems he is sick.
example_two: あの映画はとても面白いらしいですよ。(あのえいがはとてもおもしろいらしいですよ。) — I hear that movie is really interesting.
supporting_content: らしい also functions as a suffix meaning typical of / befitting: 男らしい — manly; 子供らしい笑顔 — a smile typical of a child. This is distinct from the inferential らしい. Formation: plain form + らしい (verb plain / い-adj plain / noun or な-adj stem + らしい).

title: Continuing Until — ~まで
content: まで follows a noun (time, place) or a verb plain form clause to mean until. When used with nouns it marks the end point of time or space. When used with a verb clause it means until the point when that action occurs. The action in the main clause continues up to that end point.
example_one: 6時まで働きます。(ろくじまではたらきます。) — I work until 6 o'clock.
example_two: 雨が止むまで、ここで待ちましょう。(あめがやむまで、ここでまちましょう。) — Let us wait here until the rain stops.
supporting_content: まで vs までに: まで = continuous up to the end point (duration). までに = by the deadline (completion before a point). 5時まで待つ — wait until 5. 5時までに終わらせる — finish by 5. With places: 東京まで — as far as Tokyo. Dictionary form + まで when using a verb clause: 来るまで — until someone comes.

title: By a Deadline — ~までに
content: までに follows a time noun or verb dictionary form clause to express a deadline — by the time / by a certain point. Unlike まで (continuous until), までに focuses on completing something before the deadline. The action happens at some point prior to that boundary.
example_one: 明日までに宿題を出してください。(あしたまでにしゅくだいをだしてください。) — Please hand in your homework by tomorrow.
example_two: 会議が始まるまでに資料を準備します。(かいぎがはじまるまでにしりょうをじゅんびします。) — I will prepare the materials before the meeting starts.
supporting_content: Key contrast: 5時まで — all the way until 5 (continuous). 5時までに — sometime before 5 / by 5 (completion). The verb with までに is typically a completable action (finish, submit, arrive) rather than a continuous one. Noun + までに: 試験までに覚えてください — please memorise it before the exam.

title: While / During — ~あいだ / ~ている間に
content: 間 (あいだ or あいだに) follows a plain form or て-form plus いる to express while / during the time that. When just あいだ is used, the main action continues throughout the whole period. When あいだに is used, a separate action occurs at some point within that period.
example_one: 子供が寝ているあいだ、静かにしてください。(こどもがねているあいだ、しずかにしてください。) — Please be quiet while the child is sleeping.
example_two: 日本にいるあいだに、富士山に登りたい。(にほんにいるあいだに、ふじさんにのぼりたい。) — While I am in Japan, I want to climb Mt Fuji.
supporting_content: あいだ (without に): the main clause action lasts the whole duration. あいだに (with に): something happens at a point within the interval — 留守のあいだに誰かが来た — while I was away, someone came. Compare with ながら (same subject doing two things) and とき (at the time when).

title: Wanting Things — ~ほしい
content: ほしい is an い-adjective meaning want something. It is used when the subject desires an object. The desired item is marked with が. ほしい cannot be used for wanting to do an action — use たい for that.
example_one: 新しいパソコンがほしいです。(あたらしいパソコンがほしいです。) — I want a new computer.
example_two: もっと時間がほしい。(もっとじかんがほしい。) — I want more time.
supporting_content: ほしい is an adjective: conjugates ほしくない (do not want), ほしかった (wanted), ほしくなかった. To say a third person wants something, use ほしがっている: 彼は車をほしがっています — he wants a car. Direct use of ほしい for third persons sounds presumptuous. Compare: ~たい (want to do) vs ほしい (want a thing).

title: Wanting Someone to Do Something — ~てほしい
content: て-form plus ほしい expresses a desire for someone else to do something — I want you or someone to do this. The person you want to perform the action is marked with に. This pattern expresses the speaker's wish or hope regarding another person's actions.
example_one: もっと早く来てほしいです。(もっとはやくきてほしいです。) — I want you to come earlier.
example_two: 嘘をつかないでほしい。(うそをつかないでほしい。) — I want you not to lie.
supporting_content: Negative form: ないでほしい — want someone not to. てほしい can be indirect and softer than a direct command. In formal situations, use てもらえますか or ていただけますか instead. Third person: 彼に手伝ってほしい — I want him to help. Compare: してほしい (speaker's wish) vs してもらいたい (slightly more formal).

title: Even If / Even Though — ~ても
content: て-form plus も (ても) expresses concession — even if / even though. It acknowledges a condition but states that the result is unaffected or unexpected. The main clause holds true regardless of what the ても clause describes.
example_one: 雨が降っても、試合をします。(あめがふっても、しあいをします。) — Even if it rains, we will play the match.
example_two: 疲れていても、毎日練習します。(つかれていても、まいにちれんしゅうします。) — Even though I am tired, I practice every day.
supporting_content: Formation: て-form + も. い-adj: 高くても — even if expensive. な-adj/noun: 雨でも — even if rain. Common expression: いくら〜ても — no matter how much: いくら食べても太らない — no matter how much I eat, I do not gain weight. Contrast with のに (even though — implying complaint or disappointment).

title: The Passive Voice — 〜られる
content: The passive form expresses that the subject receives an action from someone else. For u-verbs: change the final う syllable to its あ-row equivalent plus れる (書く→書かれる, 飲む→飲まれる). For ru-verbs: remove る and add られる (食べる→食べられる). する→される, くる→こられる. The agent (doer) is marked with に.
example_one: 先生に褒められました。(せんせいにほめられました。) — I was praised by the teacher.
example_two: この小説は多くの人に読まれています。(このしょうせつはおおくのひとによまれています。) — This novel is read by many people.
supporting_content: Japanese passive often has a nuance of being adversely affected: 雨に降られた — I was rained on (inconvenienced). This is the suffering passive. The passive is also used in formal writing without identifying the agent: 1868年に明治維新が行われた — the Meiji Restoration was carried out in 1868. Note: passive and potential forms are identical for ru-verbs.

title: The Causative Voice — 〜させる
content: The causative form expresses making or letting someone do something. For u-verbs: change the final う syllable to its あ-row equivalent plus せる (書く→書かせる, 飲む→飲ませる). For ru-verbs: remove る and add させる (食べる→食べさせる). する→させる, くる→こさせる. The person made or allowed to do is marked with に or を.
example_one: 子供に野菜を食べさせました。(こどもにやさいをたべさせました。) — I made the child eat vegetables.
example_two: 好きな音楽を聴かせてあげました。(すきなおんがくをきかせてあげました。) — I let them listen to music they like.
supporting_content: Distinguish make vs let from context and verb type. Intransitive verbs (行く, 来る) use を for the causee: 子供を行かせた — made the child go. Transitive verbs use に: 子供に食べさせた — made the child eat. Causative + てもらう = get permission: 休ませてもらえますか — may I take a rest?

title: The Causative-Passive — 〜させられる
content: Causative-passive combines both forms to mean to be made to do something against one's will. Formation: causative stem plus られる (u-verbs: 書かせる→書かせられる; colloquially 書かされる). For ru-verbs: 食べさせられる. する→させられる. The pattern expresses reluctance or compulsion imposed by someone else.
example_one: 子供のとき、毎日練習させられました。(こどものとき、まいにちれんしゅうさせられました。) — When I was a child, I was made to practice every day.
example_two: 残業させられて、疲れました。(ざんぎょうさせられて、つかれました。) — I was made to work overtime and I am tired.
supporting_content: Colloquial shortening for u-verbs: させられる → される (書かせられる → 書かされる). This shortening is not possible for す-ending verbs (話す → 話させられる). The causative-passive almost always carries a nuance of being compelled unwillingly, unlike the causative alone which can also mean permission.

title: Being Able to Do — ~ことができる
content: Dictionary form plus ことができる is a formal way to express ability — to be able to / can. It is equivalent in meaning to the potential form but is more explicit and often preferred in formal writing or when emphasising capability. ことができない is the negative.
example_one: 日本語を話すことができます。(にほんごをはなすことができます。) — I am able to speak Japanese.
example_two: ここで写真を撮ることができません。(ここでしゃしんをとることができません。) — You cannot take photos here.
supporting_content: ことができる vs potential form: 話せる (potential) and 話すことができる are equivalent in meaning. ことができる sounds more formal and deliberate. Common in rules and announcements: この施設では〜することができません — you cannot do this in this facility. ことができた (past) = was able to: 間に合うことができた — I was able to make it in time.

title: Expressing Ability — Potential Form
content: The potential form expresses the ability or possibility to do something — can / is able to. For u-verbs: change final う to the え-row plus る (書く→書ける, 飲む→飲める). For ru-verbs: remove る, add られる (食べる→食べられる). Irregular: する→できる, くる→こられる. The object of a potential verb is often marked with が instead of を.
example_one: ピアノが弾けます。(ピアノがひけます。) — I can play the piano.
example_two: この字が読めません。(このじがよめません。) — I cannot read this character.
supporting_content: Potential + が vs を: both are acceptable in modern Japanese, though が is more traditional with potential verbs. 日本語が話せる vs 日本語を話せる — both are common. Colloquially, ru-verb potential 食べられる is often shortened to 食べれる (ra-nuki form), but this is non-standard and should be avoided in formal contexts.

title: Only / Just — ~だけ
content: だけ follows nouns, verb plain forms, or adjectives to mean only / just / as much as. It limits the scope to exactly what is stated. だけ can appear in many positions in a sentence and is a flexible limiting expression.
example_one: 水だけ飲みました。(みずだけのみました。) — I drank only water.
example_two: できるだけ早く来てください。(できるだけはやくきてください。) — Please come as quickly as possible.
supporting_content: だけ vs しか: だけ is neutral — only without negative emphasis. しか requires a negative verb and adds a nuance of insufficiency: 水しか飲まなかった — I drank nothing but water (implying that was not enough). だけでなく — not only: 日本語だけでなく中国語も話せる — can speak not only Japanese but also Chinese.

title: Deciding to Do — ~ことにする
content: Dictionary or negative plain form plus ことにする expresses a personal decision — I have decided to / I will by my own choice. It emphasises the speaker's own volition in making the decision. The past form ことにした is commonly used to report a decision already made.
example_one: 毎日日記を書くことにしました。(まいにちにっきをかくことにしました。) — I have decided to write in my diary every day.
example_two: お酒を飲まないことにします。(おさけをのまないことにします。) — I have decided not to drink alcohol.
supporting_content: Compare with ことになる (it has been decided — by external factors). ことにする = personal, volitional decision; ことになる = result of circumstances or someone else's decision. ことにしている = I have made it a rule to: 毎朝6時に起きることにしている — I make it a rule to wake at 6 every morning.

title: It Has Been Decided — ~ことになる
content: Dictionary or negative plain form plus ことになる expresses that something has been decided or turned out to be the case as a result of external circumstances — it has been decided that / it turns out that. The decision or outcome is presented as external or natural rather than the speaker's own choice.
example_one: 来月、大阪に転勤することになりました。(らいげつ、おおさかにてんきんすることになりました。) — It has been decided that I will be transferred to Osaka next month.
example_two: この道は来年から通行止めになることになっています。(このみちはらいねんからつうこうどめになることになっています。) — This road is set to be closed to traffic from next year.
supporting_content: ことになっている = it is the rule / it is scheduled to be: 8時に集合することになっている — we are supposed to meet at 8. Compare: ことにする (personal decision) vs ことになる (external outcome). ことになった = it was decided (news); ことになっている = it is already the arrangement.

title: Called / Known As — ~という
content: Noun or plain form clause plus という plus noun means called / known as / the thing or fact that. It introduces a name, title, quotation, or definition. という functions as a relative clause connector that defines or names the following noun.
example_one: 「さくら」という名前の猫がいます。(「さくら」というなまえのねこがいます。) — There is a cat called Sakura.
example_two: 彼が来られないという話を聞きました。(かれがこられないというはなしをききました。) — I heard the story that he cannot come.
supporting_content: という can be shortened colloquially to って: さくらって猫 — a cat called Sakura. ということ = the fact that / which means: 彼が合格したということは、努力したということだ — the fact that he passed means he worked hard. といえば — speaking of: 京都といえば、お寺が有名です — speaking of Kyoto, the temples are famous.

title: Nothing But / Only — ~しか〜ない
content: しか follows a noun or quantity and requires a negative predicate to express only / nothing but with a nuance that the amount or item is insufficient or limiting. The combination しか〜ない emphasises limitation and often disappointment or resignation.
example_one: 財布に500円しかありません。(さいふに500えんしかありません。) — I only have 500 yen in my wallet (and that is not much).
example_two: この町にはコンビニしかない。(このまちにはコンビニしかない。) — There is nothing but a convenience store in this town.
supporting_content: しか vs だけ: だけ is neutral (only). しか + negative verb implies the amount is not enough or the situation is limiting in a dissatisfying way. しかない in plain form also means have no choice but to: するしかない — there is nothing to do but do it. Quantity + しか: 三人しか来なかった — only three people came.

title: Even Though (Complaint) — ~のに
content: Plain form plus のに expresses surprise, disappointment, or complaint about a result that contradicts what the speaker expected — even though / despite the fact that. It implies the speaker finds the outcome unfortunate or unreasonable. のに adds an emotional, critical nuance absent from が (but).
example_one: 一生懸命勉強したのに、試験に落ちました。(いっしょうけんめいべんきょうしたのに、しけんにおちました。) — Even though I studied hard, I failed the exam.
example_two: こんなに高いのに、全然おいしくない。(こんなにたかいのに、ぜんぜんおいしくない。) — Even though it is this expensive, it is not delicious at all.
supporting_content: のに signals the speaker's emotional reaction — usually frustration, disappointment, or criticism. Compare: けど/が (but — neutral contrast), のに (but — expressing dissatisfaction). のに is also used to express purpose before a noun: この道具は料理に使うのに便利だ — this tool is handy for cooking.

title: The More... The More — ~ば〜ほど
content: Verb ば-form plus the same verb in dictionary form, followed by ほど, expresses a proportional relationship — the more A, the more B. It can also be used with adjectives. The pattern highlights an increasing or proportional correlation between two things.
example_one: 練習すればするほど、上手になります。(れんしゅうすればするほど、じょうずになります。) — The more you practice, the better you become.
example_two: 考えれば考えるほど、難しくなります。(かんがえればかんがえるほど、むずかしくなります。) — The more I think about it, the harder it gets.
supporting_content: Formation: ば-form of verb + same verb dictionary form + ほど (すれば + する + ほど). For い-adj: 安ければ安いほど — the cheaper the better. For な-adj: 静かなら静かなほど — the quieter the better. ほど also appears independently to mean extent: 死ぬほど疲れた — tired to the point of death.

title: From One's Perspective — ~にとって
content: Noun plus にとって expresses someone's perspective, evaluation, or stance — for / from the standpoint of. It is used to express how something relates to or affects a specific person or group. にとって is followed by an evaluation or statement of significance.
example_one: 私にとって、日本語の勉強は楽しいです。(わたしにとって、にほんごのべんきょうはたのしいです。) — For me, studying Japanese is fun.
example_two: 子供にとって、遊ぶことはとても大切です。(こどもにとって、あそぶことはとてもたいせつです。) — For children, playing is very important.
supporting_content: にとって vs に: に marks indirect objects and directions; にとって specifically frames a perspective or value judgment. Compare: 彼に難しい (difficult for him — direct) vs 彼にとって難しい (difficult from his perspective). にとっては adds emphasis — as for X specifically / from X's point of view at least.

title: Depending On / By Means Of — ~によって
content: Noun plus によって has several related meanings: (1) depending on / according to — the outcome varies by the noun; (2) by means of / through — the method or agent; (3) passive agent marker in formal writing (equivalent to に in standard passive). Context determines the reading.
example_one: 人によって、考え方が違います。(ひとによって、かんがえかたがちがいます。) — Depending on the person, ways of thinking differ.
example_two: この発明によって、生活が便利になりました。(このはつめいによって、せいかつがべんりになりました。) — Thanks to this invention, life has become more convenient.
supporting_content: によって vs によると: によって = depending on / by (cause, means, agent). によると = according to (source of information): 天気予報によると — according to the weather forecast. による can pre-modify a noun: テクノロジーによる変化 — change brought about by technology. In academic writing, passive sentences use によって for the agent: 科学者によって発見された — discovered by scientists.

title: Looks Like / It Seems — ~ようだ / ~ようです
content: Plain form or noun plus ようだ expresses an inference based on direct observation or evidence the speaker has access to — looks like / it seems / it appears. ようだ is more evidence-based than らしい (indirect/hearsay) and less immediate than そうだ (pure visual impression).
example_one: 熱があるようです。(ねつがあるようです。) — It seems like you have a fever.
example_two: 外は雨が降っているようだ。(そとはあめがふっているようだ。) — It appears to be raining outside.
supporting_content: Formation: plain form + ようだ. Noun/な-adj: 静かなようだ — it seems quiet. Compare the three inference expressions: そうだ (appearance, from immediate visual impression — stem + そう), ようだ (reasonable inference from evidence — plain form + よう), らしい (indirect evidence or hearsay — plain form + らしい).

title: That Is Why / It Follows That — ~わけだ
content: Plain form plus わけだ (formal: わけです) presents a logical conclusion or natural consequence — that is why / it follows that / so naturally. It signals that the speaker has reasoned from given information to an expected result. The conclusion feels inevitable given the premise.
example_one: 毎日練習しているわけだから、上手になるはずだ。(まいにちれんしゅうしているわけだから、じょうずになるはずだ。) — Since you practice every day, it naturally follows that you will improve.
example_two: それで電車が遅れたわけですね。(それででんしゃがおくれたわけですね。) — So that is why the train was late.
supporting_content: わけだ confirms a logical deduction. Compare: わけではない (it does not mean that — negating a mistaken inference) and わけがない / わけにはいかない (there is no way / cannot possibly). Together these three form a family of わけ-based expressions.

title: It Does Not Mean That — ~わけではない
content: Plain form plus わけではない negates a logical conclusion that might otherwise be assumed — it does not mean that / it is not the case that / not necessarily. It is used to correct a misunderstanding or qualify a statement.
example_one: 嫌いなわけではないけど、得意じゃない。(きらいなわけではないけど、とくいじゃない。) — It is not that I dislike it, I just am not good at it.
example_two: 毎日勉強しているわけではありません。(まいにちべんきょうしているわけではありません。) — It is not the case that I study every day.
supporting_content: わけではない is often used with けど or が to soften a statement. Related: わけにはいかない — cannot do (due to social obligation or circumstances): 知っているのに言わないわけにはいかない — I cannot stay silent when I know.

title: There Is No Way — ~わけがない
content: Plain form plus わけがない (formal: わけがありません) asserts that something is logically impossible or completely out of the question — there is no way / it is impossible that. It expresses strong denial based on reasoning.
example_one: 彼がそんなことをするわけがない。(かれがそんなことをするわけがない。) — There is no way he would do something like that.
example_two: こんな難しい問題が解けるわけがない。(こんなむずかしいもんだいがとけるわけがない。) — There is no way I can solve a problem this difficult.
supporting_content: わけがない vs はずがない: both express that something should not be the case. わけがない is slightly more emphatic and logically absolute — denying a scenario as rationally impossible. はずがない focuses on expectation — it was not supposed to happen.

title: Must Certainly Be — ~に違いない
content: Plain form plus に違いない (formal: に違いありません) expresses strong conviction — must be / there is no doubt / I am certain that. The speaker is not guessing but asserting a near-certain conclusion from evidence or strong reasoning.
example_one: あの光は飛行機に違いない。(あのひかりはひこうきにちがいない。) — That light must be an airplane.
example_two: 彼女が合格したのは努力したからに違いない。(かのじょがごうかくしたのはどりょくしたからにちがいない。) — It must be because she worked hard that she passed.
supporting_content: に違いない expresses the speaker's confident conclusion — stronger than でしょう (probably) and ようだ (seems like). Compare: はずだ (expectation based on facts) vs に違いない (near-certainty based on reasoning or strong conviction). Negative: に違いない cannot be straightforwardly negated — use はずがない instead.

title: It Is Certain / Surely — ~に決まっている
content: Plain form or noun plus に決まっている expresses the speaker's absolute certainty or conviction — it is definitely / it must be / of course it is. It often carries a slightly dismissive or self-assured tone, implying the conclusion is obvious.
example_one: あいつが犯人に決まっている。(あいつがはんにんにきまっている。) — That person is definitely the culprit.
example_two: こんなに安いなら、品質が悪いに決まっている。(こんなにやすいなら、ひんしつがわるいにきまっている。) — If it is this cheap, the quality must obviously be poor.
supporting_content: に決まっている is more colloquial and subjective than に違いない (which is reasoned). に決まっている often reflects the speaker's personal conviction or exasperation. It is common in casual speech and monologue. Use に違いない in more measured, written contexts.

title: Nothing More Than — ~に過ぎない
content: Noun or plain form plus に過ぎない (formal: に過ぎません) means nothing more than / merely / only. It minimises or downplays the significance of something — stating that it does not exceed a certain level or category.
example_one: これは私の意見に過ぎません。(これはわたしのいけんにすぎません。) — This is merely my opinion.
example_two: まだ子供に過ぎないのに、立派な考えを持っている。(まだこどもにすぎないのに、りっぱなかんがえをもっている。) — Even though they are nothing more than a child, they have admirable ideas.
supporting_content: に過ぎない is often paired with のに or が to add contrast: に過ぎないのに〜 — even though it is merely X. Compare with だけ (only — neutral) and しか〜ない (only — dissatisfied). に過ぎない implies the speaker considers the thing small or insufficient.

title: Nothing But / Only Doing — ~ばかり
content: ばかり follows a noun or verb plain form to mean only / nothing but / just finished. Three core uses: (1) after a noun: only X / full of X; (2) after te-form: doing nothing but; (3) after past short form: just did (recently completed).
example_one: 彼女は文句ばかり言っている。(かのじょはもんくばかりいっている。) — She does nothing but complain.
example_two: 今日は食べてばかりいた。(きょうはたべてばかりいた。) — I did nothing but eat today.
supporting_content: ばかり after past short form: 起きたばかり — just woke up (moments ago). This recent-past ばかり is very common: 日本に来たばかり — just arrived in Japan. ばかりか (not only X but also Y): 彼は怠けるばかりか、他の人の邪魔もする — not only is he lazy, he also disturbs others.

title: Even / As Long As — ~さえ / ~さえ〜ば
content: さえ follows a noun or verb stem to mean even. Combined with the ば conditional, さえ〜ば means as long as / if only — expressing that a single condition is sufficient for the desired outcome.
example_one: 子供さえ知っている常識だ。(こどもさえしっているじょうしきだ。) — Even children know this common sense.
example_two: お金さえあれば、何でも買える。(おかねさえあれば、なんでもかえる。) — As long as you have money, you can buy anything.
supporting_content: さえ replaces other particles: noun + さえ (marks the extreme example). さえ + verb stem + すれば also works: 練習さえすれば上達する — if only you practice, you will improve. Compare でさえ — even X (stronger emphasis): 先生でさえ間違える — even teachers make mistakes.

title: Showing Signs Of — ~がる / ~たがる
content: Third person plus the adjective stem plus がる expresses that someone is showing outward signs of a feeling — appears to / seems to feel. たがる (want-stem + がる) means appears to want to do. These are used when describing someone else's inner state from external evidence.
example_one: 子供は甘いものをほしがっている。(こどもはあまいものをほしがっている。) — The child seems to want something sweet.
example_two: 彼は早く帰りたがっている。(かれははやくかえりたがっている。) — He appears to want to go home early.
supporting_content: Formation: adjective stem + がる (e.g., 悲しい → 悲しがる, 怖い → 怖がる). たがる: verb masu-stem + たがる (e.g., 帰りたがる). がっている is the progressive form: 悲しがっている — showing sadness. Use these only for third persons; never use them about yourself.

title: That Is How Things Are — ~ものだ
content: Plain form plus ものだ expresses a general truth, social expectation, or how things naturally are — that is the way things are / is supposed to. It can also express nostalgic recall of past habits (used to), or strong emotion/desire.
example_one: 人間はいつか死ぬものだ。(にんげんはいつかしぬものだ。) — People die eventually — that is just how things are.
example_two: 子供の頃はよく公園で遊んだものだ。(こどものころはよくこうえんであそんだものだ。) — When I was a child, I used to play in the park a lot.
supporting_content: Past short form + ものだ: nostalgic past habit (used to). Dictionary form + ものだ: natural truth or expectation. ものではない: expresses that something should not be done (it goes against common sense): そんなことを言うものではない — you should not say such a thing.

title: Although / In Spite Of — ~ものの
content: Plain form plus ものの means although / even though / in spite of the fact that. It introduces a concession — acknowledging a fact but following with a result that contrasts with or does not match the expectation. ものの carries a slightly literary nuance.
example_one: 申し込んだものの、行けなくなってしまった。(もうしこんだものの、いけなくなってしまった。) — Although I applied, I ended up being unable to go.
example_two: 日本語を勉強しているものの、なかなか上手にならない。(にほんごをべんきょうしているものの、なかなかじょうずにならない。) — Although I am studying Japanese, I am not getting better very easily.
supporting_content: ものの contrasts with のに: both mean even though, but のに carries the speaker's emotional disappointment or complaint; ものの is more neutral and descriptive. ものの is more common in written or formal contexts, while のに is more spoken.

title: There Are Times When — ~ことがある
content: Past short form plus ことがある means there are times when / sometimes do. It describes occasional or irregular occurrences — something that happens from time to time but not always. Not to be confused with ~ことがある for past experience (I have done).
example_one: 忙しくて食事をとれないことがある。(いそがしくてしょくじをとれないことがある。) — There are times when I am too busy to eat.
example_two: 仕事で深夜まで働くことがあります。(しごとでしんやまではたらくことがあります。) — There are times when I work until late at night for work.
supporting_content: Distinguish two ことがある patterns: (1) past short form + ことがある (occasional occurrence — sometimes): 遅刻することがある — I am sometimes late. (2) past short form + ことがある (past experience — have ever done): 富士山に登ったことがある — I have climbed Mt Fuji before. Tense of the verb before ことがある determines the meaning.

title: There Is No Need To — ~ことはない
content: Dictionary form plus ことはない means there is no need to / you do not have to / there is no point in. It reassures someone that an action is unnecessary or advises against worrying about something.
example_one: そんなに心配することはないよ。(そんなにしんぱいすることはないよ。) — There is no need to worry so much.
example_two: 謝ることはありません。(あやまることはありません。) — There is no need to apologise.
supporting_content: ことはない is softer than なくていい (no need to — permission not to) and more reassuring in tone. Compare: 行かなくていいよ — you do not have to go (permission); 行くことはないよ — there is no need to go (gentle advice). ことはない can also appear in the negative conditional: やらないことはない — it is not that I will not do it.

title: Whether Or Not — ~かどうか
content: Plain form plus かどうか means whether or not. It embeds a yes/no question as a noun clause within a larger sentence. かどうか can be the object of verbs like 知る (to know), 分かる (to understand), or 聞く (to ask).
example_one: 彼が来るかどうか分かりません。(かれがくるかどうかわかりません。) — I do not know whether or not he is coming.
example_two: 試験に受かったかどうかまだ聞いていない。(しけんにうかったかどうかまだきいていない。) — I have not yet asked whether or not they passed the exam.
supporting_content: かどうか vs か: かどうか is specifically for yes/no embedded questions (whether or not). か alone is used after question-word clauses: どこに行ったか教えてください — please tell me where they went. Combining: どこに行くかどうか is unusual; どこに行くか is the natural form for question-word clauses.

title: As If / Just Like — ~かのように
content: Plain form plus かのように means as if / as though / just like. It draws an analogy between the actual situation and a hypothetical one. The comparison is clearly not literally true — the speaker is using it to vividly describe how something appears or feels.
example_one: まるで夢を見ているかのような気分だった。(まるでゆめをみているかのようなきぶんだった。) — It was as if I were dreaming.
example_two: 彼はすべてを知っているかのように話す。(かれはすべてをしっているかのようにはなす。) — He speaks as if he knows everything.
supporting_content: かのように = か + の + ように. The か signals hypothetical embedding; の nominalises the clause; ように draws the comparison. まるで (as if / just like) often precedes かのように for emphasis: まるで外国にいるかのような雰囲気 — an atmosphere as if you were in a foreign country.

title: To Try To / To Attempt To — ~ようとする
content: Verb volitional form plus とする means to try to do / to attempt to / to be about to. It describes making an effort to do something, often with a sense of the action being imminent or attempted but not yet complete.
example_one: 窓を開けようとしたが、開かなかった。(まどをあけようとしたが、あかなかった。) — I tried to open the window, but it would not open.
example_two: 帰ろうとしたとき、電話が鳴った。(かえろうとしたとき、でんわがなった。) — Just as I was about to leave, the phone rang.
supporting_content: ようとする describes the moment of attempting or being on the verge of doing. It is distinct from ようにする (making an effort habitually). ようとしている = in the process of trying to: 変わろうとしている — trying to change. Often paired with が or のに to indicate the attempt was frustrated.

title: Cannot Help But Feel — ~てしょうがない / ~てたまらない
content: Te-form plus しょうがない or てたまらない expresses an overwhelming feeling or sensation that the speaker cannot control or suppress — cannot help but feel / unbearably. The feeling is so strong it is difficult to bear.
example_one: 暑くてしょうがない。(あつくてしょうがない。) — It is unbearably hot.
example_two: 早く会いたくてたまらない。(はやくあいたくてたまらない。) — I cannot wait to see you — I miss you so much.
supporting_content: Formation: te-form or くて form of い-adj + しょうがない / たまらない. しょうがない = there is nothing to be done about it (it is so much that I cannot help it). たまらない = unbearable. Both attach to adjectives and verbs expressing internal states: 気になってしょうがない — I cannot stop wondering; 嬉しくてたまらない — I am overjoyed.

title: Both... And... / What Is More — ~し〜し
content: Plain form plus し lists multiple reasons or characteristics in a non-exhaustive way — and also / moreover / for one thing... for another. し implies that the listed reasons are not the only ones — there are more unsaid reasons supporting the conclusion.
example_one: この店は安いし、おいしいし、また来たい。(このみせはやすいし、おいしいし、またきたい。) — This restaurant is cheap and delicious — I want to come again.
example_two: 疲れているし、お腹も空いているし、もう帰ります。(つかれているし、おなかもすいているし、もうかえります。) — I am tired, and hungry too, so I am going home.
supporting_content: し attaches to all predicate plain forms (verb, い-adj, な-adj + だし, noun + だし). The final し-clause is often dropped and replaced with a conclusion: 暑いし、疲れたし…(implied: so let us take a break). It is softer and more conversational than から for listing reasons.

title: Every Time — ~たびに
content: Noun plus の + たびに, or verb dictionary form plus たびに, means every time / whenever. It states that a particular result or feeling reliably occurs each time the stated action or event takes place.
example_one: 旅行のたびに、お土産を買います。(りょこうのたびに、おみやげをかいます。) — Every time I travel, I buy souvenirs.
example_two: この曲を聴くたびに、昔のことを思い出す。(このきょくをきくたびに、むかしのことをおもいだす。) — Every time I hear this song, I think of the past.
supporting_content: Formation: noun + の + たびに; verb dictionary form + たびに. たびに implies that the relationship between the action and the result is reliable and consistent — not just once, but regularly. Compare ごとに (every X interval — regularly spaced): 一時間ごとに休む — take a break every hour.

title: Thanks To — ~おかげで
content: Noun plus の + おかげで, or plain form plus おかげで, expresses gratitude or positive attribution — thanks to / owing to. It indicates that a positive outcome resulted from a particular person, thing, or circumstance. おかげで is strictly for positive outcomes.
example_one: 先生のおかげで合格できました。(せんせいのおかげでごうかくできました。) — Thanks to my teacher, I was able to pass.
example_two: 毎日練習したおかげで、上手になった。(まいにちれんしゅうしたおかげで、じょうずになった。) — Thanks to practicing every day, I improved.
supporting_content: おかげで vs せいで: おかげで is for positive results (thanks to); せいで is for negative results (because of / due to — blaming). 雨のせいで試合が中止になった — the match was cancelled because of the rain (negative). 雨が降らなかったおかげでピクニックができた — thanks to it not raining, we could have a picnic.

title: Because Of (Negative) — ~せいで
content: Noun plus の + せいで, or plain form plus せいで, assigns blame or attributes a negative outcome — because of / due to / as a result of (something bad). せいで is exclusively used for undesirable results where the cause is seen as responsible.
example_one: 電車が遅れたせいで、遅刻してしまった。(でんしゃがおくれたせいで、ちこくしてしまった。) — Because the train was delayed, I ended up being late.
example_two: 寝不足のせいで、集中できない。(ねぶそくのせいで、しゅうちゅうできない。) — Because of lack of sleep, I cannot concentrate.
supporting_content: Compare おかげで (positive attribution) vs せいで (negative attribution/blame). せいだ at the end of a sentence shifts blame: 全部あなたのせいだ — it is all your fault. せいにする = to blame something on: 天気のせいにする — to blame it on the weather.

title: Toward / In Response To — ~に対して
content: Noun plus に対して means toward / in contrast to / in response to / regarding. It marks the target of an action, attitude, comparison, or response. に対して is versatile and appears in formal writing and polite speech.
example_one: 子供に対して優しくしてください。(こどもにたいしてやさしくしてください。) — Please be kind toward the children.
example_two: 昨年に対して、今年は売り上げが増えた。(さくねんにたいして、ことしはうりあげがふえた。) — Compared to last year, sales increased this year.
supporting_content: に対して has three main uses: (1) toward / regarding (attitude or action directed at someone); (2) in contrast to (comparison between two things); (3) in response to. に対する functions as an attributive modifier before a noun: 子供に対する優しさ — kindness toward children.

title: Regarding / Concerning — ~について
content: Noun plus について means about / concerning / regarding. It marks the topic of discussion, inquiry, thought, or writing. について is one of the most common formal and informal expressions for introducing what something is about.
example_one: 日本の文化について話しましょう。(にほんのぶんかについてはなしましょう。) — Let us talk about Japanese culture.
example_two: 環境問題についてレポートを書きました。(かんきょうもんだいについてレポートをかきました。) — I wrote a report concerning environmental issues.
supporting_content: について is frequently used with verbs like 話す (talk), 考える (think), 調べる (research), 書く (write), 質問する (ask a question). についての as an attributive modifier: 日本についての本 — a book about Japan. Compare に関して (Lesson 180) which is more formal and narrow in usage.

title: Regarding (Formal) — ~に関して / ~に関する
content: Noun plus に関して means regarding / in relation to / concerning. It is more formal than について and tends to appear in official documents, academic writing, and professional speech. に関する modifies nouns: に関する + noun means a noun related to.
example_one: この件に関してご連絡いたします。(このけんにかんしてごれんらくいたします。) — I am contacting you regarding this matter.
example_two: 環境に関する問題は深刻だ。(かんきょうにかんするもんだいはしんこくだ。) — Problems related to the environment are serious.
supporting_content: に関して vs について: both mean about / regarding, but に関して is more formal and narrower in scope. について is broader and more casual — it can introduce any topic. Use に関して in formal reports, announcements, or official correspondence. に関しては adds topic emphasis: 費用に関しては問題ない — as for costs, there is no problem.

title: In the Role Of — ~として
content: Noun plus として means as / in the capacity of / in the role of. It indicates the function, role, identity, or status in which someone or something is being considered. として frames how a subject is being treated or understood.
example_one: 彼はガイドとして働いています。(かれはがいどとしてはたらいています。) — He works as a guide.
example_two: この薬は痛み止めとして使われている。(このくすりはいたみどめとしてつかわれている。) — This medicine is used as a painkiller.
supporting_content: としては adds a perspective nuance — as far as X is concerned / as X: 学生としては十分な成績だ — as grades go for a student, they are sufficient. として is distinct from に (which marks a role in becoming: 医者になる — become a doctor) vs として (already occupying the role: 医者として働く — work as a doctor).

title: Not Only But Also — ~だけでなく
content: Noun or plain form plus だけでなく means not only X but also Y. It adds an additional element that goes beyond what would be expected, signalling that the first item is not the limit.
example_one: 彼は英語だけでなく、中国語も話せる。(かれはえいごだけでなく、ちゅうごくごもはなせる。) — He can speak not only English but also Chinese.
example_two: この問題は日本だけでなく、世界全体の問題だ。(このもんだいはにほんだけでなく、せかいぜんたいのもんだいだ。) — This issue is not only Japan's problem but a problem for the entire world.
supporting_content: だけでなく vs ばかりか: both mean not only X but also Y, but ばかりか adds a stronger sense of escalation or surprise: ばかりか often implies that even more is true than expected. だけでなく is neutral addition. Common pairing: だけでなく〜も: 私だけでなく彼も行く — not only I but also he will go.

title: Not Only — ~ばかりか
content: Noun or plain form plus ばかりか means not only X but also (even more surprisingly) Y. It escalates — the second item is even more than what the first already implied. ばかりか carries a stronger sense of addition and emphasis than だけでなく.
example_one: 彼女はピアノばかりか、バイオリンもプロ並みに弾ける。(かのじょはぴあのばかりか、ばいおりんもぷろなみにひける。) — Not only can she play piano but she can also play violin at a professional level.
example_two: この店は値段が高いばかりか、サービスも悪い。(このみせはねだんがたかいばかりか、さーびすもわるい。) — This shop is not only expensive but the service is also bad.
supporting_content: ばかりか is often followed by さえ or も to reinforce the added element. ばかりでなく is a closely related variant with a nearly identical meaning — both are interchangeable in most contexts, though ばかりか is slightly more literary.

title: Far From — ~どころか
content: Noun or plain form plus どころか means far from X, in fact Y / not only X but actually the opposite of X. It sharply contradicts an expectation — what is actually true is so far from what was assumed that the assumption is almost absurd.
example_one: 休みどころか、残業が増えた。(やすみどころか、ざんぎょうがふえた。) — Far from getting days off, my overtime increased.
example_two: 日本語が上手になるどころか、忘れてきた。(にほんごがじょうずになるどころか、わすれてきた。) — Far from improving my Japanese, I have been forgetting it.
supporting_content: どころか is used when reality is the opposite of or far worse/better than the premise. Common pattern: AどころかB — not only is A not the case, but B (opposite or more extreme). どころではない: this is no time for X / X is out of the question: 遊んでいるどころではない — this is no time to be playing around.

title: In Addition To — ~上に
content: Plain form or noun plus の + 上に means in addition to / on top of / what is more. It adds another fact that reinforces the same point — both the stated fact and the additional one support the same conclusion.
example_one: 彼女は頭がいい上に、努力家だ。(かのじょはあたまがいいうえに、どりょくかだ。) — On top of being smart, she is also a hard worker.
example_two: 雨の上に、風も強かった。(あめのうえに、かぜもつよかった。) — It was raining, and on top of that, the wind was strong too.
supporting_content: 上に adds a fact of the same positive or negative tone — both reinforce each other. If the two clauses contrast, use のに or が instead. Compare: 上に (adds same-direction reinforcement), それに (and also — casual addition), さらに (furthermore — formal addition).

title: On the Other Hand / While — ~一方
content: Plain form plus 一方 (いっぽう) means on the other hand / while / as. It contrasts two concurrent or opposing situations, or indicates that one thing is happening while another is also true. 一方で is the form before a clause; 一方 alone can end a sentence.
example_one: 兄は外向的な一方、弟は内向的だ。(あにはそとむきないっぽう、おとうとはうちむきだ。) — While the older brother is outgoing, the younger brother is introverted.
example_two: 物価が上がり続ける一方、給料は変わらない。(ぶっかがあがりつづけるいっぽう、きゅうりょうはかわらない。) — While prices keep rising, salaries remain unchanged.
supporting_content: 一方 also has a second use expressing a single direction of change: 悪くなる一方だ — it keeps getting worse and worse (one-directional worsening). This one-direction use takes the dictionary form + 一方だ and is distinct from the contrast use.

title: On the Other Hand (Contrast) — ~反面
content: Plain form or noun plus の + 反面 (はんめん) means on the other hand / while / in contrast. It presents a flip side — acknowledging one quality while pointing out that the opposite quality also exists. 反面 often refers to a disadvantage paired with an advantage, or vice versa.
example_one: 彼は仕事熱心な反面、家族との時間が少ない。(かれはしごとねっしんなはんめん、かぞくとのじかんがすくない。) — While he is hardworking, he has little time with his family.
example_two: 便利な反面、人との交流が減った。(べんりなはんめん、ひととのこうりゅうがへった。) — While it is convenient, interaction with people has decreased.
supporting_content: 反面 specifically highlights an inherent tension or trade-off — one side of a coin that has two faces. Compare: 一方 (on the other hand — can contrast or show simultaneous trends); 反面 (the flip side — always implies an inherent duality). 反面 is more literary and formal than 一方.

title: After a Long Process — ~末に / ~た末に
content: Noun plus の + 末に, or verb past short form plus 末に (すえに), means after a long period of X / at the end of / finally after. It describes a result that comes at the conclusion of a prolonged effort, deliberation, or struggle.
example_one: 長い話し合いの末に、決断しました。(ながいはなしあいのすえに、けつだんしました。) — After long deliberation, we reached a decision.
example_two: 迷った末に、留学することにした。(まよったすえに、りゅうがくすることにした。) — After much deliberation, I decided to study abroad.
supporting_content: 末に implies the process leading to the conclusion was sustained and often difficult. Compare with あげく (Lesson 189) which often implies the conclusion was negative or unfortunate despite the effort. 末 + に vs 末 + の + noun: 迷った末に決めた — decided after deliberating; 迷いの末の決断 — a decision made after deliberation (noun).

title: After All That / In the End — ~あげく
content: Noun plus の + あげく, or verb past short form plus あげく (あげく), means after a long or troubled process, in the end. あげく often carries a negative connotation — the conclusion is undesirable or the effort came to nothing, though it can occasionally be neutral.
example_one: 散々迷ったあげく、何も買わなかった。(さんざんまよったあげく、なにもかわなかった。) — After all that deliberation, I bought nothing.
example_two: けんかのあげく、別れてしまった。(けんかのあげく、わかれてしまった。) — After all those fights, they ended up breaking up.
supporting_content: あげく vs 末に: 末に is neutral — a result after sustained effort; あげく often implies dissatisfaction, waste, or a bad outcome after a long struggle. あげくの果てに is an intensified form: あげくの果てに謝らなかった — and after all that, he did not even apologise.

title: As Soon As / It All Depends On — ~次第
content: Noun or verb masu-stem plus 次第 (しだい) has two distinct uses: (1) as soon as / the moment: verb masu-stem + 次第 means I will do Y as soon as X is done; (2) it all depends on / is up to: noun + 次第 means it all depends on X.
example_one: 準備ができ次第、出発します。(じゅんびができしだい、しゅっぱつします。) — We will depart as soon as preparations are complete.
example_two: 成功するかどうかはあなたの努力次第だ。(せいこうするかどうかはあなたのどりょくしだいだ。) — Whether or not you succeed depends entirely on your effort.
supporting_content: 次第 (as soon as) is formal and often written; it signals prompt action upon completion of the prior event. 次第 (depends on) expresses that the outcome is entirely contingent on the specified factor: 結果は天気次第 — the outcome depends on the weather. 次第に (gradually) is a separate adverb and should not be confused with these patterns.

title: Throughout / Spanning — ~にわたって
content: Noun plus にわたって (に渡って) means throughout / spanning / over a range of. It indicates that something extends across a wide range of time, area, or scope. にわたる modifies a noun; にわたって modifies a verb clause.
example_one: 三日間にわたって会議が続いた。(みっかかんにわたってかいぎがつづいた。) — The meeting continued for three full days.
example_two: 広い範囲にわたって被害が出た。(ひろいはんいにわたってひがいがでた。) — Damage occurred across a wide area.
supporting_content: にわたって implies full coverage across the stated range — every part is included. にわたる before a noun: 3年間にわたる研究 — research spanning three years. Compare にかけて (from X to Y — a range with endpoints) vs にわたって (throughout the whole range — total coverage).

title: Starting With — ~をはじめ / ~をはじめとして
content: Noun plus をはじめ or をはじめとして means starting with / including / X is a prime example among others. It introduces the most prominent or representative example followed by others of the same category.
example_one: 東京をはじめ、大阪や京都でも祭りが行われる。(とうきょうをはじめ、おおさかやきょうとでもまつりがおこなわれる。) — Festivals are held in Tokyo and also in Osaka and Kyoto, among other places.
example_two: 日本語をはじめとするアジアの言語は複雑だ。(にほんごをはじめとするあじあのげんごはふくざつだ。) — Asian languages, starting with Japanese, are complex.
supporting_content: をはじめ implies the named item is the chief or best-known example; the rest of the list is implied or stated. をはじめとする modifies a noun: 日本語をはじめとする言語 — languages starting with Japanese. Compare など (etc.) which simply lists without marking the first as representative.

title: Taking the Opportunity — ~をきっかけに
content: Noun plus をきっかけに (を切っ掛けに) means taking X as a trigger / starting from / as a result of / prompted by. It marks the event or experience that triggered a change or set something in motion.
example_one: 留学をきっかけに、将来の目標が変わった。(りゅうがくをきっかけに、しょうらいのもくひょうがかわった。) — Prompted by studying abroad, my future goals changed.
example_two: その失敗をきっかけに、努力するようになった。(そのしっぱいをきっかけに、どりょくするようになった。) — Taking that failure as a turning point, I started making more effort.
supporting_content: をきっかけに(して) is often used with life events: 結婚をきっかけに転職した — I changed jobs when I got married. The related expression をきっかけとして is more formal. Compare: をきっかけに (the trigger of a change) vs をもとに (based on — using as a foundation).

title: Based On — ~をもとに
content: Noun plus をもとに (を元に) means based on / drawing on / using as a foundation. It indicates that the stated noun is the source, material, or basis from which something is created, decided, or developed.
example_one: この映画は実際の話をもとに作られた。(このえいがはじっさいのはなしをもとにつくられた。) — This film was made based on a true story.
example_two: データをもとに判断してください。(でーたをもとにはんだんしてください。) — Please make your judgment based on the data.
supporting_content: をもとに vs をもとにして: both are correct; をもとにして is slightly more explicit. をもとにした + noun is the attributive form: 実話をもとにした映画 — a film based on a true story. Compare: にもとづいて (based on — rule/principle/evidence-based) vs をもとに (based on — source material or information).

title: Based On a Rule or Evidence — ~に基づいて
content: Noun plus に基づいて (に基づいて) means based on / grounded in / in accordance with. It indicates that an action, decision, or statement is founded on a rule, law, data, or established principle. More formal than をもとに.
example_one: 法律に基づいて判断する。(ほうりつにもとづいてはんだんする。) — We judge based on the law.
example_two: 事実に基づいた報告をお願いします。(じじつにもとづいたほうこくをおねがいします。) — Please provide a report grounded in facts.
supporting_content: に基づいて vs をもとに: に基づいて implies a more authoritative foundation (law, data, evidence, principle); をもとに is broader and includes inspiration, story source, or general material. に基づく before a noun: 科学に基づく治療法 — a treatment method based on science.

title: On the Occasion Of — ~に当たって / ~にあたり
content: Noun or verb dictionary form plus に当たって (にあたって) means on the occasion of / when doing / as one is about to. It marks a significant moment or undertaking and introduces a statement, preparation, or reminder appropriate to that moment. にあたり is its more formal written variant.
example_one: 新年を迎えるにあたって、目標を立てました。(しんねんをむかえるにあたって、もくひょうをたてました。) — On the occasion of welcoming the new year, I set my goals.
example_two: ご出発にあたり、いくつかご注意をお伝えします。(ごしゅっぱつにあたり、いくつかごちゅういをおつたえします。) — As you are about to depart, I would like to share a few points of caution.
supporting_content: に当たって is used for formal, often one-time occasions like ceremonies, departures, or the start of a project. It is common in speeches and official announcements. Compare に際して (Lesson 197) which has a nearly identical meaning but tends to appear in even more formal or official contexts.

title: At the Time Of — ~に際して / ~に際し
content: Noun or verb dictionary form plus に際して (にさいして) means on the occasion of / at the time of / when. It is a highly formal expression used at the start of important events, ceremonies, or significant actions. に際し is its even more compact written form.
example_one: ご契約に際して、必要書類をご準備ください。(ごけいやくにさいして、ひつようしょるいをごじゅんびください。) — At the time of signing the contract, please prepare the necessary documents.
example_two: 日本への渡航に際し、ビザが必要な場合があります。(にほんへのとこうにさいし、びざがひつようなばあいがあります。) — A visa may be required when travelling to Japan.
supporting_content: に際して is more formal than に当たって, and both are more formal than とき (when). Use に際して in official contexts: 入学に際して — at the time of enrollment; 卒業に際して — on the occasion of graduation. に際しての before a noun: 契約に際しての注意 — points to note when signing a contract.

title: Along / Following — ~に沿って
content: Noun plus に沿って (にそって) means along / following / in line with. It indicates movement along a physical path or adherence to a plan, guideline, or policy.
example_one: 川に沿って歩きました。(かわにそってあるきました。) — I walked along the river.
example_two: 計画に沿って進めてください。(けいかくにそってすすめてください。) — Please proceed in line with the plan.
supporting_content: に沿って has both physical (along a river, road, coast) and abstract (along a plan, policy, procedure) meanings. に沿う is the attributive form: 方針に沿った対応 — a response in line with the policy. Compare にしたがって (following / as X progresses) which often implies change over time; に沿って implies steady adherence.

title: Surrounding / Over the Issue Of — ~をめぐって
content: Noun plus をめぐって (を巡って) means surrounding / concerning / over the issue of. It marks a topic that is being discussed, debated, contested, or centred on — often implying dispute or concentrated attention.
example_one: 領土問題をめぐって対立が続いている。(りょうどもんだいをめぐってたいりつがつづいている。) — Confrontation continues over the territorial issue.
example_two: 遺産をめぐる争いが起きた。(いさんをめぐるあらそいがおきた。) — A dispute arose over the inheritance.
supporting_content: をめぐって often appears with words for discussion, conflict, or debate: 議論 (discussion), 対立 (conflict), 争い (dispute). をめぐる modifies a noun: 問題をめぐる議論 — debate surrounding the issue. Compare について (about — broadly introducing a topic) vs をめぐって (surrounding — indicating contested or focused attention).

title: According To / As Things Progress — ~にしたがって
content: Noun plus にしたがって (に従って) means in accordance with / following / as X increases or progresses. It expresses either (1) following a rule, instruction, or order; or (2) a proportional change — as one thing increases, another changes in tandem.
example_one: 規則にしたがって行動してください。(きそくにしたがってこうどうしてください。) — Please act in accordance with the rules.
example_two: 時間が経つにしたがって、痛みが和らいだ。(じかんがたつにしたがって、いたみがやわらいだ。) — As time passed, the pain eased.
supporting_content: Two uses: (1) following instructions (にしたがって = に従って, obeying); (2) proportional change (as X, Y also). In the proportional use, the pattern resembles につれて (Lesson 201): にしたがって is slightly more formal. Use につれて for natural accompanying changes; にしたがって for both natural changes and rule-following.

title: As / Accompanying Change — ~につれて
content: Plain form or noun plus につれて means as X changes, Y also changes / accompanying. It expresses that two things change in tandem — as one progresses or increases, the other naturally follows. につれ is a slightly more formal variant.
example_one: 年をとるにつれて、体力が落ちる。(としをとるにつれて、たいりょくがおちる。) — As you get older, your physical strength declines.
example_two: 技術が発達するにつれて、生活が便利になった。(ぎじゅつがはったつするにつれて、せいかつがべんりになった。) — As technology advanced, life became more convenient.
supporting_content: につれて implies a natural, proportional accompanying change. Compare: にしたがって (following a rule OR proportional change — more formal); にともなって (in conjunction with / as a consequence of — change is caused); ほど (the more X, the more Y — ば〜ほど pattern).

title: In Conjunction With — ~にともなって
content: Noun or plain form plus にともなって (に伴って) means in conjunction with / accompanying / as a consequence of. It marks a cause or accompanying circumstance — one change brings about another as a natural accompaniment. More formal and cause-effect than につれて.
example_one: 人口増加にともなって、住宅不足が深刻になった。(じんこうぞうかにともなって、じゅうたくぶそくがしんこくになった。) — As a result of the population increase, the housing shortage became serious.
example_two: 経済発展にともない、環境問題も増えた。(けいざいはってんにともない、かんきょうもんだいもふえた。) — With economic development, environmental problems also increased.
supporting_content: にともない is the more formal/written variant of にともなって. にともなう before a noun: 高齢化にともなう問題 — problems accompanying the aging society. Compare につれて (natural accompanying change) vs にともなって (one change causes/accompanies the other — causal nuance).

title: Formal At / In / Regarding — ~において / ~における
content: Noun plus において (に於いて) means at / in / with regard to — a formal written expression. The attributive form における modifies a following noun. Used in academic writing, news, official documents, and formal speech to indicate location, context, or field.
example_one: 現代社会において、スマートフォンは不可欠だ。(げんだいしゃかいにおいて、すまーとふぉんはふかけつだ。) — In modern society, smartphones are indispensable.
example_two: 教育における問題点を議論した。(きょういくにおけるもんだいてんをぎろんした。) — We discussed the problems in education.
supporting_content: において is the conjunctive form; における is attributive (before a noun). Both are formal equivalents of で/での. Compare: 学校で (casual/neutral) vs 学校において (formal). にて is a literary/archaic equivalent.

title: From the Standpoint Of — ~にとって
content: Noun plus にとって means for / from the perspective of / to someone. It marks whose point of view is being expressed. The evaluative or descriptive statement that follows reflects how things appear or matter to that person or group.
example_one: 子どもにとって、遊びは大切な学習だ。(こどもにとって、あそびはたいせつながくしゅうだ。) — For children, play is important learning.
example_two: 私にとって、この仕事はやりがいがある。(わたしにとって、このしごとはやりがいがある。) — For me, this job is fulfilling.
supporting_content: にとって marks the person whose perspective or interest is being expressed. にとっては/にとっても add contrastive or inclusive emphasis. Often paired with evaluative adjectives: 大切、難しい、嬉しい. Compare: に (indirect object marker) vs にとって (standpoint/perspective).

title: Towards / In Contrast — ~にたいして / ~にたいする
content: Noun plus にたいして (に対して) means towards / against / in contrast to / with respect to. Two uses: (1) target of an action or feeling; (2) contrast between two things. The attributive form にたいする modifies a following noun.
example_one: 先生の質問にたいして、学生は答えなかった。(せんせいのしつもんにたいして、がくせいはこたえなかった。) — In response to the teacher's question, the student did not answer.
example_two: 兄は社交的なのにたいして、弟は内向的だ。(あにはしゃこうてきなのにたいして、おとうとはないこうてきだ。) — The older brother is sociable, whereas the younger one is introverted.
supporting_content: (1) towards/directed at: 批判にたいして反論する; (2) contrast: A なのにたいして B (A whereas B). Compare: について (about/concerning — topic) vs にたいして (directed at target or contrasting).

title: Regardless Of — ~にかかわらず
content: Noun or paired options plus にかかわらず means regardless of / irrespective of. The result holds no matter what the preceding noun or condition is. Often paired with antonym pairs to show that neither side makes a difference.
example_one: 天気にかかわらず、試合は行われる。(てんきにかかわらず、しあいはおこなわれる。) — Regardless of the weather, the game will be held.
example_two: 経験があるかないかにかかわらず、応募できます。(けいけんがあるかないかにかかわらず、おうぼできます。) — You can apply regardless of whether you have experience or not.
supporting_content: Common paired forms: 男女にかかわらず、大小にかかわらず、成否にかかわらず. Compare: を問わず (regardless of — similar, slightly more formal for abstract paired options); にもかかわらず (despite — adversative, unexpected result).

title: Despite / Although — ~にもかかわらず
content: Noun or plain-form clause plus にもかかわらず means despite / in spite of / although. It expresses a surprising or contrary result — the outcome contradicts what one would naturally expect from the given circumstances. More formal and emphatic than のに.
example_one: 雨にもかかわらず、大勢の人が来た。(あめにもかかわらず、おおぜいのひとがきた。) — Despite the rain, a large crowd came.
example_two: 努力したにもかかわらず、試験に落ちた。(どりょくしたにもかかわらず、しけんにおちた。) — Despite studying hard, I failed the exam.
supporting_content: Follows: Noun / な-adj + であるにもかかわらず / plain verb or い-adj + にもかかわらず. Formal equivalent of のに (used for surprise or disappointment). Compare: にかかわらず (regardless of — neutral, no adversative nuance) vs にもかかわらず (despite — adversative/unexpected).

title: By / Due To / Depending On — ~によって / ~による
content: Noun plus によって has three main meanings: (1) means or agent — done by / through; (2) cause — due to / because of; (3) variation — depending on / according to. The attributive form による modifies a following noun.
example_one: この橋は地元の人々によって建てられた。(このはしはじもとのひとびとによってたてられた。) — This bridge was built by the local people.
example_two: 人によって意見が違う。(ひとによっていけんがちがう。) — Opinions differ depending on the person.
supporting_content: (1) agent in passive: 〜によって made/built by; (2) cause: 地震によって崩壊した — collapsed due to earthquake; (3) variation: 人によって — depending on the person. による before noun: 地震による被害 — damage due to the earthquake. Compare: で (simpler means/cause).

title: Over a Span Of / Throughout — ~にわたって / ~にわたる
content: Noun expressing time span, range, or area plus にわたって (に渡って) means over / spanning / throughout. Emphasises the full broad extent across time or space. The attributive form にわたる modifies a following noun.
example_one: 三年間にわたって調査が続けられた。(さんねんかんにわたってちょうさがつづけられた。) — The investigation continued over three years.
example_two: 広い範囲にわたる被害が報告された。(ひろいはんいにわたるひがいがほうこくされた。) — Damage spanning a wide area was reported.
supporting_content: Often used with: 長期間、数年、全国、広い範囲. Compare: にかけて (from X to Y — emphasises range with endpoints: 春から夏にかけて); にわたって emphasises the full extent throughout. Usually the span is notable or significant.

title: In Addition To — ~に加えて / ~に加え
content: Noun plus に加えて (に加えて) means in addition to / on top of / moreover. Introduces a second element that adds to an already-established situation. に加え is the more formal/literary variant.
example_one: 英語に加えて、中国語も話せます。(えいごにくわえて、ちゅうごくごもはなせます。) — In addition to English, I can also speak Chinese.
example_two: 寒さに加えて、強い風が吹いていた。(さむさにくわえて、つよいかぜがふいていた。) — In addition to the cold, strong winds were blowing.
supporting_content: に加えて adds a new element to an existing one (neutral in tone). Compare: だけでなく (not only X but also Y — emphasises unexpectedness of Y); のみならず (not only X but also — formal); うえに (on top of that — often negative accumulation).

title: Contrary To — ~に反して / ~に反する
content: Noun plus に反して (に反して) means contrary to / against / in opposition to. The result or reality contradicts an expectation, wish, rule, or principle. The attributive form に反する modifies a following noun.
example_one: 予想に反して、チームは勝った。(よそうにはんして、ちーむはかった。) — Contrary to expectations, the team won.
example_two: 規則に反する行為は罰せられる。(きそくにはんするこういはばっせられる。) — Acts contrary to the rules will be punished.
supporting_content: に反して follows: 予想、期待、意志、規則、命令 etc. Emphasises that reality goes against what was expected or prescribed. Compare: にもかかわらず (despite — outcome contradicts circumstance); にたいして (contrast between two things — not necessarily a contradiction of expectation).

title: On the Occasion Of — ~に際して / ~に際し
content: Noun or plain-form verb plus に際して (に際して) means on the occasion of / at the time of / upon doing. Marks a significant moment when something should be done or is being done. Formal and written; に際し is more literary.
example_one: 入学に際して、必要書類を提出してください。(にゅうがくにさいして、ひつようしょるいをていしゅつしてください。) — Upon enrollment, please submit the necessary documents.
example_two: 出発に際し、市長があいさつをした。(しゅっぱつにさいし、しちょうがあいさつをした。) — Upon departure, the mayor gave a speech.
supporting_content: に際して marks a significant event or transition as context for an action. Compare: にあたって (when/upon doing — preparation focus, slightly more common in speech); に際して focuses on the occasion itself, often for one-time formal events.

title: Regarding / Concerning — ~に関して / ~に関する
content: Noun plus に関して (に関して) means regarding / concerning / with respect to. Introduces the topic of information, discussion, or inquiry. The attributive form に関する modifies a following noun.
example_one: この件に関して、詳しく説明します。(このけんにかんして、くわしくせつめいします。) — Regarding this matter, I will explain in detail.
example_two: 環境問題に関する報告書が発表された。(かんきょうもんだいにかんするほうこくしょがはっぴょうされた。) — A report concerning environmental issues was released.
supporting_content: に関して is formal; the everyday equivalent is について. Compare: について (about/regarding — all registers); に関して (regarding — formal documents, official communication); にたいして (towards/directed at — action or feeling aimed at a target).

title: For / Considering — ~にしては
content: Noun or plain-form clause plus にしては means for / considering / given that. The speaker notes a mismatch — the result doesn't quite match what would be expected for that category or role. Often carries a subtle evaluative nuance.
example_one: 初めてにしては、上手にできた。(はじめてにしては、じょうずにできた。) — For a first attempt, you did it well.
example_two: 子どもにしては、難しい質問だ。(こどもにしては、むずかしいしつもんだ。) — For a child, that's a difficult question.
supporting_content: Expresses mild surprise or contrast with expectation based on category. Compare: にしても (even if/even for — concessive); としては (as/for — neutral standpoint); わりには (for/considering — often about degree or quantity).

title: As / In the Capacity Of — ~として
content: Noun plus として means as / in the capacity of / in the role of. Marks how a person, thing, or idea is being defined or categorised in the current context.
example_one: 彼はプロとして活躍している。(かれはぷろとしてかつやくしている。) — He is active as a professional.
example_two: この薬は痛み止めとして使われる。(このくすりはいたみどめとしてつかわれる。) — This medicine is used as a painkiller.
supporting_content: として marks role or capacity. Phrase patterns: としては (as for / from the standpoint of — 学生としては); としても (even as — 冗談としても, this is rude); にして (at the level of — literary: 三歳にしてすでに読める — already reading at age 3).

title: Judging From / Starting From — ~からして
content: Noun plus からして has two uses: (1) judging from / even — uses one element as evidence or an extreme example to make a broader point; (2) from the outset — marking where something was already the case. Often carries a critical or emphatic tone.
example_one: 態度からして、やる気がなさそうだ。(たいどからして、やるきがなさそうだ。) — Judging from his attitude, he doesn't seem motivated.
example_two: 名前からして怪しい。(なまえからしてあやしい。) — Even the name sounds suspicious.
supporting_content: からして uses one representative element to suggest a whole pattern — if even this is problematic, everything else is too. Compare: からすると/からすれば (judging from — inferring a conclusion); から見て (from the viewpoint of); だけあって (as expected — positive outcome matches status).

title: Just Because — ~からといって
content: Plain-form clause plus からといって means just because / even though. Refutes a conclusion that might be assumed from the preceding fact — the natural consequence doesn't necessarily follow. Often followed by a negative or restriction.
example_one: 忙しいからといって、食事を抜いてはいけない。(いそがしいからといって、しょくじをぬいてはいけない。) — Just because you're busy doesn't mean you should skip meals.
example_two: お金があるからといって、幸せとは限らない。(おかねがあるからといって、しあわせとはかぎらない。) — Just because you have money doesn't mean you're happy.
supporting_content: からといって refutes an automatic logical connection. The からといって clause gives the apparent reason; the main clause counters with a restriction or negation. Often combined with: わけではない、とは限らない、てはいけない. Compare: からには (now that/since — commits to a conclusion).

title: Judging From / From One's Perspective — ~からすると / ~からすれば
content: Noun plus からすると or からすれば means judging from / from the perspective of / based on. Uses observable evidence or someone else's standpoint to draw an inference or describe how things appear. The two forms are interchangeable; からすれば is slightly more conditional.
example_one: 様子からすると、試験はうまくいったようだ。(ようすからすると、しけんはうまくいったようだ。) — Judging from how things look, the exam seems to have gone well.
example_two: 子どもからすれば、それは理不尽に思えるだろう。(こどもからすれば、それはりふじんにおもえるだろう。) — From a child's perspective, that would probably seem unreasonable.
supporting_content: からすると/からすれば can mark: (1) evidence for inference (様子からすると); (2) someone's viewpoint (子どもからすれば). Compare: からして (judging from — critical tone, single evidence point); から見て (from the viewpoint of — more literal/positional).

title: There Is a Risk / Fear That — ~おそれがある
content: Plain-form verb or noun plus のおそれがある (恐れがある) means there is a risk / fear / possibility of something bad happening. Expresses concern about a negative potential outcome. Formal; common in news, official warnings, and written Japanese.
example_one: 台風が上陸するおそれがあります。(たいふうがじょうりくするおそれがあります。) — There is a risk that the typhoon will make landfall.
example_two: この薬は副作用のおそれがある。(このくすりはふくさようのおそれがある。) — This medicine carries the risk of side effects.
supporting_content: Used exclusively for negative or undesirable possibilities. Compare: かもしれない (might — neutral, any register); かねない (might happen — negative, slightly less formal); 可能性がある (there is a possibility — neutral, can be positive). おそれがある = formal/written negative risk.

title: Cannot Bring Oneself To — ~かねる
content: Verb masu-stem plus かねる means cannot bring oneself to / find it difficult or impossible to. The speaker is psychologically or circumstantially unable to act — often due to politeness, ethics, or emotional difficulty. Used to politely decline or express reluctance.
example_one: その条件では承諾しかねます。(そのじょうけんではしょうだくしかねます。) — Under those conditions, I cannot bring myself to agree.
example_two: 彼の気持ちは理解しかねる部分がある。(かれのきもちはりかいしかねるぶぶんがある。) — There are parts of his feelings I find hard to understand.
supporting_content: Verb stem + かねる = polite refusal or reluctance. かねます is the polite form used in formal/business contexts: 対応しかねます (we cannot accommodate). Compare: にくい (hard to do — practical difficulty); づらい (hard to do — psychological); かねない (might happen — opposite meaning, do not confuse).

title: Might Possibly / Could Well — ~かねない
content: Verb masu-stem plus かねない means might possibly / could well / is not impossible. Expresses concern that a negative outcome might occur — the speaker warns that such a result is plausible and should not be dismissed. Always refers to undesirable possibilities.
example_one: このまま放置すれば、大事になりかねない。(このままほうちすれば、おおごとになりかねない。) — If left as is, it could well become a big problem.
example_two: 冗談も言い方によっては傷つけかねない。(じょうだんもいいかたによってはきずつけかねない。) — Even a joke, depending on how it's said, could hurt someone.
supporting_content: Verb stem + かねない = negative possibility the speaker fears. It wouldn't be surprising if this bad thing happened. Compare: かねる (cannot bring oneself to — present refusal/reluctance, opposite direction); おそれがある (risk that — more formal, used in official contexts).

title: Cannot Help But — ~ないではいられない / ~ずにはいられない
content: Verb negative ない-form plus ではいられない means cannot help but / cannot stop oneself from. The speaker is compelled by emotion or instinct — the action is irresistible. ずにはいられない is a more literary/formal equivalent (replace ない with ず; する → せずにはいられない).
example_one: この映画を見ると、泣かないではいられない。(このえいがをみると、なかないではいられない。) — Watching this film, I can't help but cry.
example_two: あまりにひどいので、文句を言わずにはいられなかった。(あまりにひどいので、もんくをいわずにはいられなかった。) — It was so terrible, I couldn't help but complain.
supporting_content: Double negative: ないでは + いられない (can't be in the state of not doing). Compare: てしまう (end up doing — often regret or involuntary); てならない (can't help feeling — mainly emotions/sensations); てたまらない (unbearably — desire or sensation, not actions).

title: Without — ~なしに / ~なしには
content: Noun plus なしに means without — performing or achieving something without a necessary condition or step. なしには emphasises that without X the following is impossible (often a negative result). A formal and emphatic alternative to なしで.
example_one: 許可なしに入ることはできない。(きょかなしにはいることはできない。) — You cannot enter without permission.
example_two: 努力なしには、成功はありえない。(どりょくなしには、せいこうはありえない。) — Without effort, success is impossible.
supporting_content: なしに / なしには follow nouns only (formal written). Compare: ないで (without doing — casual verb-based: 食べないで出かけた); ずに (without doing — literary: 食べずに出かけた); なしで (without — casual noun-based: 彼なしで = without him).

title: Although / While — ~ながら(も)
content: Verb masu-stem, adjective, or noun plus ながら(も) means although / despite / while. Marks a concessive relationship — the main clause holds true even though the ながら clause might suggest otherwise. も intensifies the concession. More formal than のに or けど.
example_one: 狭いながらも、居心地のいい部屋だ。(せまいながらも、いごこちのいいへやだ。) — Although it's small, it's a comfortable room.
example_two: 知りながら(も)、黙っていた。(しりながらも、だまっていた。) — Although knowing, he kept silent.
supporting_content: Used with: adj stem + ながら (小さいながら)、V-stem + ながら (知りながら)、noun + ながら (素人ながら — for a layman). Note: the same ながら can mean simultaneous action (聴きながら勉強する) — context determines which reading applies.

title: While / Even Though — ~つつ(も)
content: Verb masu-stem plus つつ means while (simultaneous action, literary) or even though / despite (concessive, often with も). The concessive use expresses acting contrary to one's own awareness or feelings. The simultaneous use parallels ながら but is more formal/literary.
example_one: 悪いと知りつつ(も)、やめられなかった。(わるいとしりつつも、やめられなかった。) — Even though I knew it was wrong, I couldn't stop.
example_two: 彼は迷いつつ、決断した。(かれはまよいつつ、けつだんした。) — While hesitating, he made a decision.
supporting_content: Two uses: (1) simultaneous/literary ながら: 考えつつ歩く; (2) concessive with も: 知りつつも — even though knowing. The concessive use is the distinctly N2 pattern. Compare: ながらも (concessive — similar, slightly more everyday); つつある is a separate pattern meaning in the process of.

title: In the Process Of / Progressively — ~つつある
content: Verb masu-stem plus つつある means to be in the process of / to be gradually doing. Expresses an ongoing change or progression currently in the middle of transitioning. Common in formal writing, news, and reports; typically used with verbs of change.
example_one: 世界の気温は上昇しつつある。(せかいのきおんはじょうしょうしつつある。) — The world's temperature is rising progressively.
example_two: その技術は急速に発展しつつある。(そのぎじゅつはきゅうそくにはってんしつつある。) — That technology is developing rapidly.
supporting_content: つつある = ongoing gradual change (formal/literary). Compare: ている (ongoing state — all registers); てくる (change toward present); ていく (change moving away from present). つつある is used specifically for gradual development or transition, common in written/formal contexts.

title: The Moment / Just As — ~たとたん(に)
content: Verb ta-form (past) plus とたん(に) (途端に) means the moment / just as / just when. An unexpected result occurs immediately after the action. The surprise or immediacy is the key nuance — the second event was not planned or expected.
example_one: ドアを開けたとたん、猫が飛び出した。(どあをあけたとたん、ねこがとびだした。) — The moment I opened the door, the cat leaped out.
example_two: 席についたとたんに、電話が鳴った。(せきについたとたんに、でんわがなった。) — Just as I sat down, the phone rang.
supporting_content: た-form + とたん(に) = unexpected event right after. The main clause event is usually sudden and often beyond the speaker's control. Compare: やいなや (as soon as — even more immediate, literary); なり (as soon as — immediate, continuous state follows); と同時に (at the same time — simultaneous, no surprise). とたん is not used for the speaker's own intentional next action.

title: Only After Doing — ~て初めて
content: Verb te-form plus はじめて means only after doing X did I realise / was I able to Y. The te-form clause is a prerequisite — until that action or experience happened, the main clause was not possible or known. Emphasises that X was the turning point.
example_one: 失って初めて、その大切さがわかった。(うしなってはじめて、そのたいせつさがわかった。) — Only after losing it did I realise how precious it was.
example_two: 外国に住んで初めて、日本のよさを感じた。(がいこくにすんではじめて、にほんのよさをかんじた。) — Only after living abroad did I appreciate the good points of Japan.
supporting_content: て初めて marks a transformative threshold: the te-clause experience enables or reveals the main clause (often a realisation or new ability). Compare: てから (after doing — sequential, no special enabling nuance); てこそ (precisely by doing — even more emphatic that only this method works).

title: Unless / Until After Doing — ~てからでないと
content: Verb te-form plus からでないと means unless/until after doing X, Y cannot happen. States a prerequisite — X must be completed first. The negative structure (でないと / でなければ) is key: the condition is that X has not yet been completed.
example_one: 確認してからでないと、返事はできません。(かくにんしてからでないと、へんじはできません。) — Unless I confirm first, I cannot give an answer.
example_two: 食べてからでなければ、出発できない。(たべてからでなければ、しゅっぱつできない。) — We can't leave unless we've eaten first.
supporting_content: て + から + でないと/でなければ: must complete X before Y is possible. Casual equivalent: してからじゃないと. Compare: てはじめて (only after X — emphasis on transformative realisation); てからでないと (sequential prerequisite — cannot proceed without completing X first).

title: Even If It Means — ~てでも
content: Verb te-form plus でも means even if it means (doing that) / even at the cost of. Expresses strong determination — the speaker is willing to do something extreme or costly in order to achieve a goal. The te-form clause names the extreme measure.
example_one: 借金をしてでも、夢を叶えたい。(しゃっきんをしてでも、ゆめをかなえたい。) — Even if it means going into debt, I want to fulfil my dream.
example_two: 徹夜してでも、この仕事を終わらせる。(てつやしてでも、このしごとをおわらせる。) — Even if I have to pull an all-nighter, I'll finish this work.
supporting_content: て + でも = strong determination at a price. The te-clause names the sacrifice. Compare: ても (even if — general concession); てでも adds determination and cost. Also: てまでも (even going so far as to — slightly stronger/formal: 嘘をついてまでも欲しくない).

title: From the Fact That — ~ことから
content: Plain-form clause plus ことから means from the fact that / because / since. Introduces observable evidence or a factual basis from which a conclusion, label, or characterisation is drawn. Often used to explain the origin of a name or characteristic.
example_one: この山は富士山に似ていることから、小富士と呼ばれている。(このやまはふじさんににていることから、こふじとよばれている。) — Because it resembles Mt. Fuji, this mountain is called Ko-Fuji.
example_two: 初戦に負けたことから、チームの士気が低下した。(しょせんにまけたことから、ちーむのしきがていかした。) — From the fact that they lost the first match, the team's morale dropped.
supporting_content: ことから introduces evidence or a factual basis — often for a name origin, characterisation, or result. Compare: ので (because — direct cause, polite); から (because — direct cause, general); ことから is more formal and used when the basis is an observable fact.

title: Without Doing — ~ことなく
content: Plain-form verb plus ことなく means without doing / without ever doing. States that something was achieved or continued without the ことなく action occurring. Formal and literary; usually refers to sustained absence of an action over time.
example_one: 彼は文句を言うことなく、仕事を続けた。(かれはもんくをいうことなく、しごとをつづけた。) — He continued working without complaining.
example_two: 休むことなく、三時間歩き続けた。(やすむことなく、さんじかんあるきつづけた。) — Without resting, I continued walking for three hours.
supporting_content: V-plain + ことなく = without ever doing (formal). Compare: ないで (without doing — casual); ずに (without doing — literary); ことなく is more formal and emphasises the full sustained absence of the action throughout an extended period.

title: To One's... — ~ことに(は)
content: Adjective or verb ta-form plus ことに(は) means to one's [emotion] / much to one's [feeling]. Introduces the speaker's emotional reaction to a situation. Common expressions: 驚いたことに、嬉しいことに、残念なことに、困ったことに.
example_one: 驚いたことに、彼が戻ってきた。(おどろいたことに、かれがもどってきた。) — To my surprise, he came back.
example_two: 残念なことに、試合は中止になった。(ざんねんなことに、しあいはちゅうしになった。) — To my disappointment, the match was cancelled.
supporting_content: ことに(は) frames the whole sentence with an emotional stance. Common emotion words: 驚いた、嬉しい、悲しい、残念な、困った、おかしい. Compare: ことか (how... — exclamatory about degree/frequency, different pattern); ことに simply introduces the emotional lens.

title: How (Much) / How Many Times — ~ことか
content: Wh-word (何度、どれほど etc.) plus ことか expresses deep emotion or strong feeling about frequency or degree. It is an exclamatory expression conveying surprise, wonder, frustration, or sadness — a rhetorical exclamation rather than a real question.
example_one: あなたに何度お願いしたことか。(あなたになんどおねがいしたことか。) — How many times have I asked you!
example_two: どれほど心配したことか、わかってほしい。(どれほどしんぱいしたことか、わかってほしい。) — I want you to understand just how much I worried.
